chapters.title: Phagocyte function & deficiencies
chapters.subtitle: Follow one cell, one signal, one enzyme at a time — from the moment a pathogen is detected to the moment it's destroyed.

stations.label: Station 00
stations.title: First step in the innate cascade
stations.subtitle: Before any one cell moves.
stations.stage: Orientation · the whole module in one frame
stations.cardTitle: Nine stages, four defects, one diagram.
stations.tagline: Before we zoom into any single cell, see the whole cascade at once.
stations.story:
The innate immune response looks complicated the first time you meet it — macrophages, cytokines, selectins, integrins, phagolysosomes, respiratory bursts, four overlapping disease names. It's easy to get lost in the molecules and miss the shape.

But the shape is simple: a bacterium breaks in, a cell detects it, signals go out, a vessel responds, a neutrophil is recruited, it finds the bug, eats it, kills it, and the mess gets cleaned up. Nine stages, always in that order, each dependent on the one before.

This module is that chain — zoomed in. Every station ahead is a closer look at one of these nine zones, or at a specific way the chain can break. When your integrins don't work, the neutrophil can't grip and stage 5 collapses (LAD). When your phagosomes can't fuse with lysosomes, stage 7 collapses (Chédiak). When your NADPH oxidase is broken, stage 8 collapses (CGD). When your myeloperoxidase is missing, stage 8 is weakened (MPO deficiency).

Keep this map in your head. Every time we introduce a new molecule, ask yourself — which stage is this? Every time we introduce a new disease, ask — which link is broken?

diagrams.caption: The whole module on one page. Nine numbered stages (1 breach → 9 cleanup), with four clinical defects marked where their chapter stations fit into the chain.

flow.text: A bacterium breaches the skin.
flow.subtext: This is the trigger for everything downstream. Until a barrier is crossed, the innate system is at rest.
flow.insight: Breach = the entry point that starts the cascade

flow.text: A tissue macrophage detects it.
flow.subtext: TLRs on the macrophage recognize pathogen patterns — LPS, flagellin, dsRNA. Instant pattern recognition, no training needed.
flow.insight: Macrophage TLR catches the PAMP

flow.text: It fires cytokines.
flow.subtext: NF-κB drives transcription of IL-1, TNF, and IL-8. These flood outward toward the nearest vessel.
flow.insight: NF-κB → IL-1, TNF, IL-8 released

flow.text: The nearby vessel lights up.
flow.subtext: Endothelial cells upregulate selectins and ICAM-1 — the adhesion molecules that will catch circulating neutrophils.
flow.insight: Endothelium expresses selectins + ICAM-1

flow.text: A neutrophil rolls, sticks, and crosses.
flow.subtext: Selectins slow it → chemokines activate its integrins → the integrins grab ICAM-1 → it squeezes through the endothelial gap.
flow.insight: Roll → activate → adhere → transmigrate

flow.text: It follows the trail.
flow.subtext: Now in the tissue, it migrates up the chemokine gradient — straight toward the bacterium that started all this.
flow.insight: Chemotaxis up the gradient to the source

flow.text: It eats the bug.
flow.subtext: Pseudopods wrap around the bacterium. It's pulled inside into a phagosome, which fuses with a lysosome to form a phagolysosome.
flow.insight: Phagocytosis → phagolysosome formation

flow.text: It kills it.
flow.subtext: NADPH oxidase ignites the respiratory burst. O₂⁻ → H₂O₂ → HOCl. The bacterium is chemically destroyed.
flow.insight: Respiratory burst: O2- → H2O2 → HOCl

flow.text: The mess gets cleaned up.
flow.subtext: Macrophages return to phagocytose the dying neutrophils and debris. The tissue resolves. Repair begins.
flow.insight: Macrophages clear debris → resolution

flow.text: And four things can break this chain.
flow.subtext: LAD breaks stage 5 (no grip). Chédiak breaks stage 7 (no fusion). CGD breaks stage 8 (no oxidants). MPO deficiency weakens stage 8 (no HOCl). Each is a station in this module.
flow.insight: Defects: LAD · Chédiak · CGD · MPO

stations.bridge: A bacterium has crossed a break in the skin. Over the next several minutes, events you just watched in the diagram will play out — in a strict molecular order, each step dependent on the one before. To lock that sequence in, pin down which step is the very first.
stations.vignette: A bacterium crosses a break in the skin and enters the underlying tissue. Within seconds, the innate immune cascade begins, and over the next several hours it destroys the invader and repairs the damage. These events follow a strict molecular order — not in parallel, but sequential — with each step dependent on the one before.
stations.stem: Which of the following is the first step in this cascade?

choices.text: Neutrophils firmly adhere to endothelial ICAM-1
A tissue macrophage recognizes a pathogen-associated molecular pattern
NADPH oxidase generates superoxide inside the phagolysosome
Cytokines upregulate selectins on nearby endothelial cells
The neutrophil migrates along a chemokine gradient in the tissue

stations.explanation:
The innate immune cascade runs top-down from a single moment: a tissue macrophage detects that something is wrong. That's the recognition event — a pathogen-associated molecular pattern (PAMP) binding to a pattern-recognition receptor like TLR4. Nothing else happens until that binding occurs.

Every other choice is a real event in the cascade, but downstream:

  • D (endothelial selectin upregulation) only happens after the macrophage has already signaled — via IL-1 and TNF released downstream of detection.
  • A (neutrophil firm adhesion) comes after the neutrophil has rolled on selectins and been activated by chemokines.
  • E (chemotaxis in tissue) requires that the neutrophil has already transmigrated out of the vessel.
  • C (respiratory burst) is the late, killing step — it only happens after the neutrophil has engulfed the bug.

This single principle — that innate immunity is a sequence, not a soup — is what organizes every station ahead. Every chapter is a zoom-in on one segment of the big picture you just saw. Every disease you'll meet is a broken link in this chain.
stations.whatsNext: You just saw the whole flight path. Before we pick one specific detector and watch it fire, let's meet the full family of sensors — every pattern they recognize, every compartment they watch.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: PRR compartment → PAMP matching
stations.subtitle: PAMPs, PRRs, and the compartments they watch.
stations.stage: A zoom-out from TLR4 to the whole detection system
stations.previously: You just saw the cascade from a distance — detection, signaling, recruitment, killing. Before we dive into one specific detector in the next station, it helps to see the full map of how the body recognizes not-self. Different microbes, different sensors, different compartments — but all ending in the same alarm.
stations.tagline: Every class of microbe leaves a molecular fingerprint. The body has a dedicated sensor for each one.
stations.story:
Every microbe carries molecules that healthy human cells do not. Gram-negative bacteria have LPS. Gram-positive bacteria have peptidoglycan. Bacteria have flagellin on their flagella and unmethylated CpG DNA in their chromosomes. Viruses carry double-stranded RNA and single-stranded RNA as their genome. Fungi have mannose-rich carbohydrates on their surface.

These are called PAMPs — pathogen-associated molecular patterns. They are the molecular fingerprints of not-self.

The body's job is to detect them. It does this with a family of dedicated receptors called PRRs — pattern recognition receptors. Every PRR is hardwired to recognize one class of PAMP. And different PRRs sit in different places, watching different compartments of the cell.

The location of the receptor tells you what it is watching for. Surface TLRs watch the extracellular world and catch bacteria swimming by. Endosomal TLRs watch the inside of engulfed vesicles — where viral genomes appear after a virus is taken up. Cytoplasmic sensors (NLRs, RLRs) watch the cytoplasm itself — where an intracellular pathogen lives.

Every one of these, when it fires, converges on the same downstream cascade you've already learned: NF-κB drives cytokine gene transcription. Different input, same output. That's why the map of sensors is worth memorizing even if the downstream story is one you already know.

diagrams.caption: One cell, three compartments, three classes of sensor. The PAMP you need to detect determines where the receptor must live.

flow.text: A microbe is made of molecular patterns your cells don't have.
flow.subtext: LPS, flagellin, dsRNA, mannose — every class of microbe has its own signature.
flow.insight: PAMPs = molecular fingerprints of not-self

flow.text: The body has dedicated sensors for each class.
flow.subtext: Called PRRs — pattern recognition receptors. Four main families: TLRs, NLRs, RLRs, CLRs.
flow.insight: PRRs = hardwired sensors for each PAMP class

flow.text: Surface TLRs: watch the extracellular world.
flow.subtext: TLR4 → LPS · TLR5 → flagellin · TLR2 → peptidoglycan/lipoteichoic acid. All catch bacteria before they get in.
flow.insight: Surface TLRs catch extracellular bacteria — TLR4 = LPS, TLR5 = flagellin, TLR2 = peptidoglycan

flow.text: Endosomal TLRs: watch the inside of engulfed vesicles.
flow.subtext: TLR3 → dsRNA · TLR7/8 → ssRNA · TLR9 → CpG DNA. All nucleic acids — all viral genomes exposed after endocytosis.
flow.insight: Endosomal TLRs catch viral genomes after uptake — TLR3 = dsRNA, TLR7/8 = ssRNA, TLR9 = CpG DNA

flow.text: Cytoplasmic sensors: watch for things that made it inside.
flow.subtext: NOD1/2 → intracellular peptidoglycan · RIG-I / MDA5 → cytoplasmic viral RNA. The last line of detection.
flow.insight: Cytoplasmic sensors catch invaders inside the cell — NLRs for bacteria, RLRs for viral RNA

flow.text: Every PRR ends the same way: NF-κB fires, cytokines are made.
flow.subtext: Different input, same output. That's why the cascade from Stations 02–03 applies to all of them.
flow.insight: All PRRs converge on NF-κB — same cytokine output

stations.bridge: A child keeps getting recurrent herpes simplex encephalitis. Every few years, HSV reactivates and spreads into the CNS. His immune workup is mostly normal — except for one receptor. This is a real syndrome, and the receptor that's missing tells you exactly which compartment HSV lives in.
stations.vignette:
A 7-year-old boy presents with his second episode of herpes simplex virus encephalitis in three years. Between episodes he is well. His total antibody levels, T-cell counts, and neutrophil function are all normal. Targeted genetic testing reveals a loss-of-function mutation in a pattern recognition receptor that normally detects HSV replication intermediates in the endosome.

HSV, a double-stranded DNA virus, produces double-stranded RNA as it replicates inside infected cells. After phagocytosis by plasmacytoid dendritic cells, this dsRNA is exposed within the endosome.
stations.stem: A mutation in which of the following receptors best explains this patient's susceptibility to recurrent HSV encephalitis?
stations.explanation:
TLR3 lives in the endosome and recognizes double-stranded RNA, which is a replication intermediate of many viruses — including HSV. TLR3 deficiency is a well-described cause of recurrent herpes simplex encephalitis in otherwise healthy children, because TLR3 is the key sensor for HSV in the CNS.

TLR4 (A) recognizes LPS from gram-negative bacteria. TLR5 (C) recognizes flagellin. NOD2 (D) is a cytoplasmic sensor of intracellular peptidoglycan — mutations cause Crohn's disease, not viral susceptibility. CLR / Dectin-1 (E) recognizes fungal β-glucans.

The clue chain: endosome + dsRNA + HSV = TLR3.
stations.whatsNext:
You now know the full map: the patterns, the receptors, the compartments. From the next station onward we stop zooming out and start zooming in. One pattern. One receptor. One signal. One cell.

We'll start with the most famous pairing in innate immunity — LPS (the toxic outer coat of gram-negative bacteria) binding TLR4 on a tissue macrophage.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: LPS → TLR4 activation
stations.subtitle: The alarm fires.
stations.previously: You just saw the four families of sensors — TLRs, NLRs, RLRs, CLRs — and how each one lives in a different compartment. Now we pick the most famous one and watch it fire in real time. A tissue macrophage is about to meet a gram-negative bacterium, and TLR4 is about to recognize LPS.
stations.cardTitle: Something is wrong in the tissue.
stations.tagline: Before any cell moves, something has to detect the enemy.
stations.story:
The innate immune system has built-in detectors — ancient, genetically hardwired receptors that recognize the molecular signatures of microbes. They don't need to be "trained" like antibodies. They don't need prior exposure. They're born into every one of your macrophages, ready from day one.

The most important family is called Toll-like receptors, or TLRs. Each one recognizes a specific class of microbial pattern — a lipid, a sugar, a strip of nucleic acid — common to many germs but absent from healthy human cells.

The single most famous TLR is TLR4, and its target is lipopolysaccharide (LPS) — the outer-membrane component of gram-negative bacteria. You might hear LPS called endotoxin.

When LPS binds TLR4 on a nearby macrophage, the cell lights up. Everything else in this chapter — the vessel changes, the neutrophil recruitment, the engulfment, the chemistry — all of it is downstream of this single moment of recognition.

diagrams.caption: LPS studs the outer membrane of gram-negative bacteria. When one drifts to a macrophage, TLR4 catches it — and the cascade begins.

flow.text: Macrophages have built-in sensors.
flow.subtext: Ancient. Hardwired. Every macrophage, from day one.
flow.insight: Macrophages have built-in sensors

flow.text: These sensors are called Toll-like receptors.
flow.subtext: Short: TLRs.
flow.insight: The sensors are called TLRs

flow.prompt: In one sentence — what's a TLR?

flow.text: Each TLR recognizes one pattern germs have, but you don't.
flow.subtext: Lipids, sugars, fragments of microbial nucleic acid.
flow.insight: Each TLR = one microbial pattern

flow.text: One matters more than all the others on Step 1:
flow.subtext: TLR4
flow.insight: TLR4 is the one that gets tested

flow.text: TLR4 watches for LPS — the outer wall of gram-negative bacteria.
flow.subtext: You might hear LPS called endotoxin. Same thing.
flow.insight: TLR4 binds LPS (= endotoxin)

flow.question: Quick check: what does TLR4 bind to?

feedback.correct: Yes. That's it. TLR4 + LPS = the moment the alarm fires.
feedback.wrong: Not quite — antibodies and complement come into play later. The detector binding the microbial pattern here is TLR4, and what it binds is LPS.

flow.text: LPS binds TLR4. The macrophage lights up.
flow.insight: Binding activates the macrophage

flow.text: Everything else in this chapter — vessel changes, neutrophils, chemistry — all starts here.
flow.subtext: This single moment of recognition.
flow.insight: This moment starts all of innate inflammation

stations.bridge: An older man has come into the emergency department with a perforated appendix. Gram-negative bacteria are spilling into his peritoneum — and every macrophage patrolling the tissue is about to do exactly what we just described.
stations.vignette:
A 70-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, chills, and right lower quadrant abdominal pain. His temperature is 39°C (102.2°F), and laboratory testing reveals a leukocyte count of 21,000/mm³ with a left shift. Imaging demonstrates a perforated appendix with a surrounding intra-abdominal abscess.

During the acute inflammatory response, bacterial cell wall lipopolysaccharides stimulate neutrophil activation.
stations.stem: These lipopolysaccharides most directly bind to which of the following?
stations.explanation:
LPS is the archetypal TLR4 ligand. Cytokine receptors bind cytokines like IL-1 or TNF — the output of this system, not the input. G protein-linked receptors handle chemokines and histamine. Ion channels are for neurotransmitters. Nuclear receptors bind steroid-like molecules that enter the cell.

Only the TLR family is built to recognize microbial patterns like LPS. And TLR4 specifically is the one that handles it.
stations.whatsNext:
TLR4 has fired. But a receptor firing at the cell surface isn't enough on its own — the signal has to get translated into action inside the macrophage. Into gene expression. Into the manufacturing of the cytokines that will soon pour out of this cell and change everything.

And for that, there's one transcription factor that matters more than all the others.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: NF-κB: the transcription factor downstream of TLR4
stations.subtitle: NF-κB switches on.
stations.stage: From surface receptor to nuclear transcription
stations.previously: TLR4 on a macrophage has just bound LPS. The receptor is firing — but the inflammatory response hasn't started yet. Something inside the cell has to translate that alarm into action.
stations.tagline: One transcription factor is about to turn on the whole inflammatory response.
stations.story:
When LPS binds TLR4, a signaling cascade runs inside the macrophage and ends at a transcription factor called NF-κB. This is arguably the single most important transcription factor in all of inflammation.

NF-κB's job is to enter the nucleus and turn on the genes for inflammatory cytokines — TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6 — the molecules that drive fever, vasodilation, and the whole systemic inflammatory response.

In severe gram-negative sepsis, this cascade runs so hard and so long that the patient develops septic shock: hypotension, organ failure, the works. NF-κB is the gas pedal of inflammation — and in sepsis, it's pressed to the floor.

diagrams.caption: Signal flows down: active TLR4 at the membrane → NF-κB in the cytoplasm → translocation into the nucleus → binds promoter → cytokine genes transcribe.

flow.text: TLR4 just fired. The signal runs down into the cell.
flow.subtext: The alarm has to reach the nucleus. Here's how.
flow.insight: Signal travels from TLR4 down into the cell

flow.text: A signaling cascade runs through the cytoplasm.
flow.subtext: Kinase after kinase, carrying the alarm inward.
flow.insight: Signaling cascade carries the alarm inward

flow.text: The cascade ends at one protein: NF-κB.
flow.subtext: The most important transcription factor in all of inflammation.
flow.insight: The cascade ends at NF-κB

flow.prompt: Tell your partner: what does a transcription factor actually do?

flow.text: NF-κB enters the nucleus and turns on cytokine genes.
flow.subtext: Straight to the DNA. Transcription starts.
flow.insight: NF-κB enters nucleus → turns genes ON

flow.text: The genes it switches on: TNF-α · IL-1 · IL-6.
flow.subtext: The three big inflammatory cytokines.
flow.insight: Outputs: TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6

flow.question: Quick check: which cytokines does NF-κB activate?

feedback.correct: Right. Those three drive fever, vasodilation, and the whole systemic response.
feedback.wrong: Those are immune molecules too, but not the ones transcribed here. NF-κB turns on TNF-α, IL-1, and IL-6 — the cytokines of fever and inflammation.

flow.text: These cytokines drive fever, vasodilation, and the systemic response.
flow.subtext: Everything that feels like being sick.
flow.insight: Cytokines → fever + vasodilation

flow.text: NF-κB is the gas pedal of inflammation.
flow.subtext: In sepsis, it's pressed to the floor. Hypotension. Organ failure. Septic shock.
flow.insight: Sepsis = NF-κB firing too hard, too long

stations.bridge: A patient in gram-negative septic shock. His macrophages are manufacturing TNF by the gram — and every one of those cytokine genes was switched on by a single transcription factor.
stations.vignette: A patient with a severe gram-negative bloodstream infection develops fever, hypotension, and signs of septic shock. Laboratory evaluation shows markedly increased production of tumor necrosis factor (TNF) by activated macrophages following exposure to bacterial endotoxin. This response requires activation of transcription of multiple pro-inflammatory cytokine genes.
stations.stem: LPS-mediated activation of transcription most likely depends on which of the following transcription factors?
stations.explanation:
The master inflammatory switch.

c-ras is a proto-oncogene involved in cell growth signaling — not a transcription factor for cytokines. p53 is the tumor suppressor guarding against DNA damage. Rb is a cell cycle regulator. VP-16 (etoposide) isn't a transcription factor at all — it's a chemotherapy drug that inhibits topoisomerase II.

The distractors are all "important proteins," but only NF-κB has the specific job of driving inflammatory gene transcription downstream of TLR4.
stations.whatsNext:
But wait — if NF-κB is so ready to go, why isn't it always firing? In a resting cell, it's sitting right there in the cytoplasm, already made. Something has to be holding it back until the signal arrives.

Meet the gatekeeper.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: IκB degradation releases NF-κB
stations.subtitle: The brake comes off.
stations.previously: NF-κB is ready to drive cytokine production. But it's being physically held in the cytoplasm, kept out of the nucleus. Something has to let it go — and the way that happens is the kind of biochemical detail board exams love.
stations.tagline: Phosphorylation is the trigger. Destruction is the mechanism. Release of NF-κB is the result.
stations.story:
In a resting macrophage, NF-κB is already made. The protein is sitting in the cytoplasm, ready to do its job — but it's being physically restrained. An inhibitor called IκB (inhibitor of κB) is clamped onto NF-κB, covering the part that would bind DNA and preventing it from entering the nucleus.

Think of NF-κB as a race car idling in the driveway. Engine on. Ready to go. And IκB is the parking brake.

When a signal arrives — from TLR4, IL-1 receptor, or TNF receptor — a kinase called IKK gets activated and does one thing: it adds a phosphate group to IκB. Phosphorylation is a death tag. It marks IκB for ubiquitination and destruction by the proteasome.

Once IκB is gone, NF-κB is free. Nucleus. DNA. Genes on.

The precise answer to "what does IκB do?" is: IκB releases NF-κB after undergoing phosphorylation.

diagrams.caption: Three states left to right: IκB clamped (covers NF-κB) → IκB phosphorylated and ubiquitin-tagged → IκB destroyed, NF-κB free to enter the nucleus.

flow.text: NF-κB is already made in a resting cell.
flow.subtext: Sitting in the cytoplasm, ready to go.
flow.insight: NF-κB is pre-made, waiting in cytoplasm

flow.text: But something is holding it back.
flow.subtext: An inhibitor called IκB is clamped onto NF-κB.
flow.insight: IκB clamps onto NF-κB

flow.text: Think of NF-κB as a race car. Engine on. IκB is the parking brake.
flow.subtext: That's why it's stuck in the cytoplasm — not yet active.
flow.insight: IκB = the parking brake on NF-κB

flow.prompt: Why can't NF-κB just do its job immediately?

flow.text: A signal arrives — from TLR4, IL-1R, or TNF-R.
flow.subtext: A kinase called IKK gets activated.
flow.insight: Signal → activates kinase IKK

flow.text: IKK phosphorylates IκB. This is the key step.
flow.subtext: A phosphate lands on IκB. That's a death tag.
flow.insight: IKK phosphorylates IκB (death tag)

flow.text: The tag recruits ubiquitin → the proteasome destroys IκB.
flow.subtext: P + Ub = shipped for destruction.
flow.insight: Phosphorylated IκB is destroyed

flow.question: IκB's role is to...

feedback.correct: Exactly. IκB is the one that GETS phosphorylated, and its destruction is what releases NF-κB. That's the entire mechanism.
feedback.wrong: Careful with directions: IκB is on the RECEIVING end — IKK phosphorylates IT. Its destruction is what frees NF-κB. IκB releases NF-κB after undergoing phosphorylation.

flow.text: NF-κB is free. Nucleus. DNA. Genes on.
flow.subtext: The parking brake is gone. The race starts.
flow.insight: IκB destroyed → NF-κB free → genes ON

stations.bridge: A man with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis is having a flare. IL-1 is flooding his synovium. NF-κB signaling is running at full tilt — which means IκB is doing something very specific.
stations.vignette: A 62-year-old man with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis presents with acute swelling and pain of the right knee. Arthrocentesis shows 48,000 leukocytes/mm³ with 90% neutrophils; Gram stain is negative. Synovial fluid analysis demonstrates markedly elevated interleukin-1 (IL-1). In response to IL-1 signaling, synovial macrophages increase transcription of interleukin-6 (IL-6) as part of the inflammatory cascade. IL-1 induces IL-6 through the nuclear factor-kappa B (NF-κB) signaling pathway.
stations.stem: Which of the following best describes the role of IκB in this pathway?

choices.text: Facilitates proteolytic cleavage of NF-κB dimers
Translocates to the nucleus after undergoing phosphorylation

stations.explanationTitle: Answer · D · Releases NF-κB after undergoing phosphorylation
stations.explanation:
The causal chain: IL-1 binding → IKK activation → IKK phosphorylates IκB → phosphorylated IκB is ubiquitinated and degraded → NF-κB is released → NF-κB translocates to the nucleus and turns on IL-6 transcription.

IκB doesn't attach to the cytokine receptor (A) — that's the receptor's own job. IκB doesn't cleave NF-κB (B); NF-κB is activated by release, not cleavage. IκB doesn't phosphorylate NF-κB (C); it's IκB that gets phosphorylated, by IKK. And IκB does not translocate to the nucleus (E) — it's destroyed, and NF-κB is the one that travels.
stations.whatsNext:
TLR4 → IKK → IκB destroyed → NF-κB freed. You know the mechanism. You know why the parking brake metaphor works.

Now for the payoff. The inflammatory cascade begins in the tissue surrounding an infection. The first cells to arrive — neutrophils — don't just swim there. They execute a sequence of steps so precise it has its own terminology.

bank.label: Bank 01 · Pyelonephritis
bank.vignette: A 72-year-old man is admitted to the hospital with fever, chills, and flank pain for 24 hours. His temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F). Urinalysis shows numerous leukocytes and bacteria, and blood cultures later grow Escherichia coli. During the innate immune response, bacterial products stimulate cytokine release, including interleukin-1 (IL-1). Activation of the IL-1 receptor leads to downstream NF-κB signaling.
bank.stem: Which of the following best describes the role of IκB in this pathway?

choices.text: Facilitates proteolytic cleavage of NF-κB dimers
Translocates to the nucleus after undergoing phosphorylation

bank.explanation: Same molecular story, different clinical stage: a gram-negative UTI climbing up to the kidneys. The IL-1 receptor fires, IKK activates, IκB is phosphorylated and destroyed, and freed NF-κB drives the inflammatory gene program.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Selectin upregulation on activated endothelium
stations.subtitle: Selectins slow it down.
stations.previously: Cytokines from the activated macrophage have reached the nearby blood vessels. Now it's the endothelium's turn to respond.
stations.cardTitle: The vessel wall puts out sticky patches.
stations.tagline: A neutrophil racing through the bloodstream is about to slow down.
stations.story:
Here's the thing about blood vessels: they're sealed tubes. Neutrophils race through them at high speed, bumping against the endothelial wall but never sticking. If they never stop, they can never get out.

When the cytokines from the previous station — TNF, IL-1 — reach the endothelium, the endothelial cells begin displaying sticky molecules called selectins on the inner wall of the vessel, facing the bloodstream.

Selectins are weak grippers. They don't clamp down — they brush. A neutrophil catches one, sticks briefly, releases, catches the next. The result is that the neutrophil stops racing and starts rolling, tumbling slowly along the vessel wall like a stone rolling downhill.

diagrams.caption: The full leukocyte adhesion cascade. The selectin question lives in panel 2 — the rolling step. The other panels preview what comes next.

flow.text: Blood vessels are sealed tubes.
flow.subtext: Neutrophils race through, bumping the walls but never sticking. Never stopping.
flow.insight: Neutrophils race through vessels without sticking

flow.text: The macrophage's cytokines — TNF, IL-1 — reach the endothelium.
flow.subtext: The vessel wall is about to change.
flow.insight: Cytokines reach the endothelium

flow.text: The endothelium puts out selectins — sticky patches on the lining.
flow.subtext: Facing the bloodstream. Facing the racing neutrophils.
flow.insight: Endothelium puts out selectins (luminal side)

flow.text: Selectins are weak grippers. They don't clamp — they brush.
flow.subtext: A neutrophil catches one, sticks briefly, releases, catches the next.
flow.insight: Selectins = WEAK grippers (brush, not clamp)

flow.prompt: Why are selectins called WEAK grippers?

flow.text: The neutrophil stops racing. It starts rolling.
flow.subtext: Slow tumbling along the vessel wall — like a stone rolling downhill.
flow.insight: Neutrophil slows to rolling motion

flow.text: On the exam: 'loose, reversible adhesion' = rolling = selectins.
flow.subtext: That phrase always maps to selectins. Memorize the wording.
flow.insight: Exam trigger: 'loose, reversible adhesion' = selectins

flow.question: Quick check: which molecule mediates the rolling step?

feedback.correct: Yes. Selectins = rolling. Integrins and ICAM-1 are the strong grippers that come in later for firm arrest.
feedback.wrong: Integrins and ICAM-1 are the STRONG grippers — they come later. Selectins are the WEAK ones that do the initial rolling.

flow.text: Rolling is the setup. A rolling neutrophil still moves.
flow.subtext: It has to stop completely. A stronger grip comes next station.
flow.insight: Rolling is the setup — firm arrest comes next

stations.bridge: The rolling step, seen in real time. Researchers inject Streptococcus pyogenes into mouse dermis and watch what appears on the endothelium within minutes.
stations.vignette: In a laboratory study of acute bacterial infection, Streptococcus pyogenes is injected into the dermis of a mouse model. Within minutes, circulating neutrophils are observed slowing down and intermittently attaching to the vascular endothelium near the site of infection before firmly adhering and migrating into the tissue. Analysis of endothelial cells from the affected vessels shows increased expression of a surface molecule responsible for loose, reversible adhesion of leukocytes.
stations.stem: Which of the following is most likely upregulated on these endothelial cells?
stations.explanation:
"Loose, reversible adhesion" = rolling = selectins (especially E-selectin and P-selectin on endothelium). That's the whole answer.

Integrins on leukocytes mediate firm adhesion, not rolling. ICAM-1 is the endothelial ligand for integrins — again, firm adhesion, not loose. Prostacyclin is a vasodilator and antiplatelet molecule. Thrombomodulin is an anticoagulant endothelial receptor.
stations.whatsNext:
Rolling is a start. But a rolling neutrophil is still moving — and if it keeps rolling, it'll roll right past the infection. It has to stop. Firmly. Completely.

For that, the endothelium needs a stronger grip.

bank.label: Bank 01 · Finger Splinter
bank.vignette: A 28-year-old man presents with a painful abscess on his finger after a splinter injury. Leukocytes localize to the site of inflammation by adhering to endothelial cells through binding of endothelial adhesion molecules to ligands on leukocytes.
bank.stem: Which of the following molecules is most responsible for mediating this initial leukocyte adhesion to the endothelium?
bank.explanation: Same biology, different anatomical setting. Cadherins hold epithelial cells together in tissues — not involved in leukocyte rolling. ECM proteins like collagen are structural scaffolding. G proteins are intracellular signaling. Proteoglycans are ground substance. Only selectins do the initial rolling step.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: ICAM-1 and firm leukocyte adhesion
stations.subtitle: The cell locks in.
stations.previously: The neutrophil is rolling, loosely attached, drifting slowly along the vessel wall. Now the endothelium adds a stronger kind of adhesion molecule — and the neutrophil comes to a full stop.
stations.cardTitle: The neutrophil slams to a halt.
stations.tagline: No more rolling. The cell is locked in position, ready to exit.
stations.story:
Once a neutrophil is rolling, the same cytokines that put selectins on the endothelium drive display of a second kind of molecule: ICAM-1 (intercellular adhesion molecule-1). Think of ICAM-1 as a Velcro patch bolted onto the vessel wall.

Meanwhile, the rolling neutrophil has its own Velcro hooks, called integrins — proteins like LFA-1 and Mac-1. Integrins are normally in a low-affinity state. When chemokines from the tissue reach the rolling neutrophil, they flip the integrins into a high-affinity state.

Activated integrins grab ICAM-1. The bond is strong. The neutrophil slams to a halt.

No more rolling. The cell is locked in place, ready to squeeze through the vessel wall and exit into the tissue.

diagrams.caption: Same cascade as the previous station — but this time the focus is panel 3, where activated integrins on the neutrophil grip ICAM-1 on the endothelium.

flow.text: The neutrophil is still rolling on selectins.
flow.subtext: Loose, reversible adhesion. Slow tumbling. Still moving.
flow.insight: Starting point: neutrophil rolling on selectins

flow.text: The same cytokines put out a new molecule: ICAM-1.
flow.subtext: Intercellular adhesion molecule-1 — a Velcro patch bolted to the wall.
flow.insight: Endothelium now displays ICAM-1

flow.text: The neutrophil has integrins — LFA-1 and Mac-1.
flow.subtext: But they're in a low-affinity state. Folded up. Asleep.
flow.insight: Neutrophil carries integrins (LFA-1, Mac-1) in low-affinity state

flow.prompt: Why would the body keep integrins inactive by default?

flow.text: Chemokines from the tissue reach the rolling neutrophil.
flow.subtext: A specific activation signal — not just any cytokine.
flow.insight: Chemokines from tissue reach the neutrophil

flow.text: Chemokines flip the integrins to high-affinity.
flow.subtext: A conformational change. The integrin unfolds — stands up — ready to grab.
flow.insight: Chemokines → integrins become high-affinity (conformational change)

flow.question: ICAM-1 on the endothelium is directly responsible for which step?

feedback.correct: Right. ICAM-1 is the anchor point — it's the molecule that firmly sticks the neutrophil to the endothelium. Activation is upstream (chemokines). Transmigration (PECAM-1) is next.
feedback.wrong: Careful: activation is done by chemokines, and transmigration is done by PECAM-1 in the next step. ICAM-1's specific job is ADHERENCE — firmly anchoring the neutrophil to the endothelium.

flow.text: The activated integrin grabs ICAM-1. The bond is strong.
flow.subtext: No more brushing — this is a locked grip.
flow.insight: Integrin + ICAM-1 = firm, locked grip

flow.text: The neutrophil stops completely. Locked in place.
flow.subtext: No more rolling. Ready to squeeze through the vessel wall next.
flow.insight: Firm adhesion complete → transmigration is next

stations.bridge: A patient with bacterial pneumonia. Cytokines have reached the pulmonary vessels. ICAM-1 is now being displayed on the endothelial wall — but what does it actually do for the neutrophil?
stations.vignette: A 54-year-old man develops acute bacterial pneumonia. Inflammatory cytokines released at the site of infection cause endothelial cells in postcapillary venules to upregulate surface adhesion molecules. As a result, circulating neutrophils slow down and bind firmly to the vascular endothelium before exiting the bloodstream.
stations.stem: The increased expression of ICAM-1 is directly responsible for which of the following neutrophil functions?
stations.explanation:
ICAM-1 is specifically the "stop and stick" molecule.

Activation is upstream — that's chemokines flipping integrins to high-affinity state. Chemotaxis happens later, once the neutrophil is out of the vessel and following a chemical trail. Demargination is cells leaving the vessel walls and re-entering circulation — the opposite of what's happening here. Transmigration, the squeezing-between-endothelial-cells step, is mediated by PECAM-1.
stations.whatsNext:
So far, the cascade has gone perfectly. Selectins on the endothelium, integrins on the neutrophil, a tight grip, firm adhesion, ready to exit.

But what if the integrin is missing? What if the neutrophil arrives at the rolling step, fails to firm-adhere, and just keeps drifting past? The clinical picture is specific — and weirder than you'd guess.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: CD18 defect → no pus, delayed cord separation
stations.subtitle: No CD18. No grip.
stations.previously: In a normal patient, integrins grab ICAM-1 and the neutrophil stops. But what happens when the integrin molecule itself is defective — when the patient is born without the piece that holds the cell in place?
stations.tagline: A patient with no pus, dramatic neutrophilia, and a cord that never fell off.
stations.story:
What happens if the firm-adhesion step is broken? The clinical picture is specific — and weirder than you'd guess.

The disease is leukocyte adhesion deficiency (LAD), most commonly caused by a mutation in CD18 — the β2 integrin subunit that teams with CD11 to form LFA-1 and Mac-1. Without CD18, the neutrophil has no functional integrin, no firm adhesion, no transmigration.

The clinical picture:

  • The umbilical cord doesn't separate on time. Normal separation is day 10–14. In LAD, it's delayed past 3 weeks because neutrophils can't migrate into the stump.
  • Infections with no pus. Pus is made of neutrophils. No neutrophils in tissue = no pus, even in severe infection.
  • Dramatic neutrophilia on CBC. The cells are produced normally but can't leave the blood. They pile up in circulation while tissues go undefended.

Infections without pus + neutrophilia + delayed cord separation. That tetrad is LAD. The defect is CD18 — the piece that couldn't grab ICAM-1.

diagrams.caption: Same cascade as Stations 4 and 5, but with the firm-adhesion step broken. The neutrophil drifts past the ICAM-1 it cannot grip.
What you see at the bedside — and how each finding traces back to the broken cascade above.

flow.text: Normally, the integrin on the neutrophil grabs ICAM-1 on the wall.
flow.subtext: Firm adhesion = integrin + ICAM-1. That grip is how the cell stops.
flow.insight: Normal firm adhesion: integrin grabs ICAM-1

flow.text: The integrin has two subunits: CD11 + CD18.
flow.subtext: CD11 is the α-chain. CD18 is the β-chain (β2). They snap together to form LFA-1 and Mac-1.
flow.insight: Integrin = CD11 (α) + CD18 (β2) subunits

flow.text: In LAD, CD18 is broken.
flow.subtext: The β2 subunit is mutated. Without it, the integrin can't form properly.
flow.insight: LAD defect: CD18 (β2 subunit)

flow.text: The rolling neutrophil can't firm-adhere. It drifts past ICAM-1.
flow.subtext: Without CD18, there's no grip. The cell keeps moving through the bloodstream.
flow.insight: No CD18 → no grip → no firm adhesion → no exit

flow.prompt: What would you expect to see on this patient's CBC?

flow.text: Neutrophils pile up in the blood. Tissue is undefended.
flow.subtext: They're made normally by bone marrow — but they can't leave the vessel.
flow.insight: Dramatic neutrophilia: cells pile up in blood (can't exit)

flow.text: Clinical clues: no pus at infections, delayed cord separation.
flow.subtext: Pus is made of neutrophils. No neutrophils reach tissue = no pus. Normal cord separation is 10–14 days; LAD > 3 weeks.
flow.insight: Bedside: no pus + delayed umbilical cord (>3 weeks)

flow.question: Which molecule is defective in LAD type 1?

feedback.correct: Exactly. CD18 pairs with CD11 to form LFA-1 and Mac-1. Without CD18, the whole β2-integrin family collapses — no firm adhesion, no transmigration.
feedback.wrong: Look at what the neutrophil is MISSING: its own grip. ICAM-1 and selectins are on the endothelium and are fine in LAD. The broken piece is the neutrophil's integrin — specifically the β2 subunit CD18.

flow.text: The full LAD tetrad — this is what they want you to recognize.
flow.subtext: Delayed cord separation · infections without pus · marked neutrophilia · recurrent bacterial infections.
flow.insight: LAD tetrad: delayed cord + no pus + neutrophilia + infections

stations.bridge: Here's the LAD presentation we just described, in a 5-month-old boy. The delayed cord separation. The pus-less infections. The sky-high white count. Every piece of the tetrad.
stations.vignette: A 5-month-old boy is evaluated for recurrent bacterial infections since birth. His umbilical cord separated at 5 weeks of age. Physical examination reveals poorly healing skin lesions without significant purulence despite active infection. Laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 42,000/mm³ with marked neutrophilia. Functional testing of his neutrophils demonstrates impaired adhesion to vascular endothelium and failure to migrate to sites of infection.
stations.stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's immunologic defect?

choices.text: Absent CD40 ligand on T cells

stations.explanation:
Classic LAD triad: delayed cord separation + infections with no pus + peripheral neutrophilia. The integrins can't bind ICAM-1 on endothelium, so neutrophils can't firm-adhere and can't exit the vessel.

C3 deficiency would cause severe recurrent pyogenic infections but with pus formation preserved. NADPH oxidase defects cause CGD — catalase-positive infections with granuloma formation, not neutrophil extravasation failure. CD40L deficiency causes hyper-IgM syndrome. Lysosomal trafficking defects cause Chédiak-Higashi — coming up very soon.
stations.whatsNext:
Assume the cascade works. The neutrophil has rolled, stuck, transmigrated, and is now in the tissue. It follows a chemical trail through the interstitium, right to the bacterium. They're face to face.

Now comes the part where the neutrophil actually eats the bacterium. And that turns out to require something surprising — a rebuilding of the cell's own skeleton in real time.
stations.nextBtn: Face to face with the bacterium →

bank.label: Bank 01 · Beta-2 Integrin Block
bank.vignette: Researchers are developing a monoclonal antibody that blocks CD11a/CD18 (LFA-1) on neutrophils to reduce excessive inflammation. In preclinical studies, the drug prevents neutrophils from adhering tightly to endothelial cells even when ICAM-1 is highly upregulated.
bank.stem: Which step in neutrophil recruitment is most directly impaired by this drug?
bank.explanation: Blocking the β2 integrin (CD18) component of LFA-1 prevents the integrin from binding ICAM-1. This is the firm adhesion step. Rolling (selectin-dependent) is preserved.

bank.label: Bank 02 · Recognizing the Pattern
bank.vignette: A 2-year-old girl has had recurrent otitis media, periodontitis, and poorly healing skin infections since infancy. Her parents report that her umbilical cord fell off at 4 weeks of age. Physical examination reveals multiple skin infections without purulent drainage. Peripheral blood leukocyte count is 38,000/mm³ with 85% neutrophils.
bank.stem: A defect in which of the following is most likely responsible for this patient's condition?
bank.explanation: CD18 deficiency → LAD-1. All the classic features are here: delayed cord separation, recurrent infections without pus, marked peripheral neutrophilia.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Actin-dependent phagocytosis
stations.subtitle: Swallowed whole.
stations.previously: The neutrophil has reached the bacterium. Now comes the moment of engulfment — and it turns out this requires something you might not expect.
stations.cardTitle: The cell reshapes itself in real time.
stations.tagline: Phagocytosis isn't passive. It's a cytoskeletal construction project.
stations.story:
Phagocytosis — literally "cell eating" — is the neutrophil's signature move. The cell reaches out membrane projections called pseudopods, wraps them around the bacterium, and pulls the whole thing inside into a sealed bubble called a phagosome.

But here's the detail that matters enormously: this reaching-and-wrapping movement requires the cell to rebuild its skeleton on the fly. And the cell's skeleton is made of actin filaments — long chains of actin protein that assemble and disassemble on demand.

Without actin polymerization, the cell cannot push its membrane outward. No pseudopods. No engulfment. No phagocytosis.

diagrams.caption: Watch the cell reshape itself across four frames. The driver strip below shows the cause-and-effect: actin polymerization is what physically pushes the membrane outward.

flow.text: The neutrophil is face to face with the bacterium.
flow.subtext: Transmigrated, migrated through tissue, arrived. Now what?
flow.insight: Neutrophil arrives at the bacterium in tissue

flow.text: The neutrophil extends pseudopods — membrane projections.
flow.subtext: Arms of cell membrane reaching outward toward the bacterium.
flow.insight: Pseudopods = membrane projections reaching out

flow.text: Pseudopods wrap around the bacterium.
flow.subtext: Like hands folding around a ball. Extending, curling, meeting on the far side.
flow.insight: Pseudopods curl around the bacterium

flow.prompt: What's pushing the membrane outward? Something has to power the reach.

flow.text: Inside: actin filaments assemble, pushing the membrane out.
flow.subtext: Actin monomers polymerize into long chains. The chains physically drive the membrane forward.
flow.insight: Actin polymerization drives pseudopod extension

flow.text: The bacterium is sealed inside a phagosome.
flow.subtext: The two pseudopods meet on the far side. Membrane pinches off. Bacterium is now INSIDE the cell.
flow.insight: Bacterium sealed inside a phagosome

flow.question: A compound blocks actin polymerization in neutrophils. Which function is most impaired?

feedback.correct: Correct. No actin, no pseudopods, no engulfment. Phagocytosis needs the cytoskeleton to push membrane outward.
feedback.wrong: Reach for the cytoskeletal step: pseudopod formation — that's what visually bends the membrane outward. Without actin, the reach fails. That's phagocytosis.

flow.text: The bacterium is trapped — but still alive.
flow.subtext: Phagocytosis only gets it INSIDE. Killing is a separate step.
flow.insight: Engulfment is the setup — killing comes next

flow.text: But first — what happens when the cell can get to the bacterium, engulf it — and then fail?
flow.subtext: Engulfment is perfect. Killing is broken. That's the next clinical disease.
flow.insight: Next: a disease where killing fails (Chédiak-Higashi)

stations.bridge: In the lab: a compound is added to a neutrophil culture that blocks actin polymerization. A very specific cellular function stops working — and only that one.
stations.vignette: Researchers are studying neutrophil function in vitro. A novel compound is added to the culture that inhibits actin filament assembly by preventing actin monomers from polymerizing. The investigators observe impaired engulfment of bacteria by these cells.
stations.stem: Which of the following leukocyte functions is most likely to be inhibited by this compound?
stations.explanation:
All five choices involve getting something into a cell — but only phagocytosis requires the dramatic membrane extension that depends on actin.

Mannose receptor binding is molecular recognition; no cytoskeleton needed. Clathrin-coated pits and receptor-mediated endocytosis use clathrin, not actin, for small vesicles. Pinocytosis ("cell drinking") doesn't need the big pseudopod machinery.
stations.whatsNext:
The bacterium is inside. Sealed in a phagosome. Helpless.

But engulfment isn't killing. For killing, the phagosome needs to fuse with the lysosomes — the granules packed with destructive enzymes. And that fusion, it turns out, can itself fail.
stations.nextBtn: What if the granules can't fuse? →

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: LYST mutation → failed phagolysosome fusion
stations.subtitle: Giant granules fail.
stations.previously: The bacterium has been engulfed. The phagosome is sealed. Now it needs to fuse with lysosomes for killing to begin. But what if those lysosomes are defective?
stations.cardTitle: Silvery hair. Giant granules. Infections that won't stop.
stations.tagline: A single gene defect that you can sometimes spot from across the room.
stations.story:
What about cells that get to the tissue, phagocytose the bacterium, form a perfect phagosome — and then fail to kill what's inside?

That's Chédiak-Higashi syndrome. The defect is in the LYST gene (lysosomal trafficking regulator), which controls how lysosomes fuse with other vesicles. When LYST is broken, vesicles clump together into huge, dysfunctional mega-granules.

The consequences sprawl across body systems because lysosome-like vesicles exist everywhere:

  • In neutrophils: the phagosome can't fuse properly with lysosomes. Recurrent pyogenic infections. On smear, giant cytoplasmic granules visible under light microscopy.
  • In melanocytes: melanosomes fuse abnormally. Result: partial albinism — silvery hair, pale skin.
  • In neurons: progressive peripheral neuropathy.
  • In platelets: dense granule defects → mild bleeding.

Partial albinism + giant cytoplasmic granules + recurrent infections + peripheral neuropathy. That's the Chédiak-Higashi quartet.

diagrams.caption: Normal trafficking on the left; LYST-defective trafficking on the right. Same starting parts, different fusion outcome.
One molecular defect, four cell types, four clinical findings.

flow.text: Normally, the phagosome fuses with lysosomes.
flow.subtext: Lysosomes dump their digestive enzymes into the phagosome — and kill the bug.
flow.insight: Normal killing = phagosome + lysosome fusion

flow.text: That fusion is controlled by the LYST gene.
flow.subtext: LYST = LYSosomal Trafficking regulator. It tells lysosomes where to go and when to fuse.
flow.insight: LYST = the lysosomal trafficking regulator

flow.text: In Chédiak-Higashi, LYST is broken.
flow.subtext: Vesicles don't know where to go. They clump together into one huge dysfunctional granule.
flow.insight: LYST defect → vesicles clump into megagranules

flow.text: In the neutrophil: the phagosome can't fuse properly.
flow.subtext: The bacterium is trapped inside — but never gets the killing enzymes. It survives.
flow.insight: Phagosome can't fuse → bacterium trapped but alive

flow.prompt: Lysosome-like vesicles exist all over the body — where else would you expect problems?

flow.text: Melanocytes use the same fusion machinery.
flow.subtext: Melanosomes can't deliver pigment → silvery-gray hair, pale skin, partial albinism.
flow.insight: Melanocytes affected → silvery hair, partial albinism

flow.question: Which gene is defective in Chédiak-Higashi?

feedback.correct: Exactly. LYST controls lysosomal trafficking — without it, vesicles clump into megagranules and phagosomes can't fuse properly.
feedback.wrong: CD18 → LAD (firm adhesion failure). CYBB → CGD (no respiratory burst). Chédiak-Higashi is specifically the LYST gene controlling lysosomal fusion.

flow.text: Plus: peripheral neuropathy and mild bleeding.
flow.subtext: Neurons and platelet dense granules use the same fusion machinery too.
flow.insight: Also affected: neurons (neuropathy) + platelets (bleeding)

flow.text: The Chédiak-Higashi quartet.
flow.subtext: Partial albinism · giant granules on smear · recurrent pyogenic infections · peripheral neuropathy.
flow.insight: Quartet: albinism + giant granules + infections + neuropathy

stations.bridge: A 4-year-old boy with silvery hair and pale skin. Giant granules on his blood smear. Mild peripheral neuropathy. Every piece of the Chédiak-Higashi quartet is there — now pick the mechanism.
stations.vignette: A 4-year-old boy is evaluated for recurrent pyogenic skin and respiratory infections. On examination, he has silvery-gray hair, pale skin, and light-colored irises. Peripheral blood smear demonstrates large cytoplasmic granules within neutrophils. Neurologic examination reveals mild peripheral neuropathy.
stations.stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's condition?
stations.explanation:
Chédiak-Higashi syndrome. The combination of silvery hair + giant granules on smear + recurrent infections + neuropathy is essentially diagnostic.

NADPH oxidase defect → CGD (coming in two stations). CD18 defect → LAD (previous station). MPO deficiency → Candida susceptibility (coming up). IFN-γ receptor defect → mycobacterial susceptibility (Chapter 4).
stations.whatsNext:
Assume the phagolysosome forms correctly. The bacterium is sealed in the kill chamber. Now the neutrophil fires its most destructive weapon — a chemical one.

This is the assembly line that makes bleach inside your cells.

bank.label: Bank 01 · The Giant-Granule Clue
bank.vignette: A 3-year-old girl with recurrent pneumonia is noted to have silvery-gray hair and photosensitivity. Light microscopy of a peripheral blood smear reveals large abnormal cytoplasmic granules within neutrophils. Cultures from recurrent skin infections have grown Staphylococcus aureus multiple times.
bank.stem: Which of the following is the most likely underlying defect?

choices.text: Failure of phagolysosome fusion due to defective lysosomal trafficking
Inability to generate superoxide in the phagolysosome
Inability to produce hypochlorous acid from hydrogen peroxide
Inability of leukocytes to adhere to endothelium

bank.explanation: Silvery hair + giant granules + recurrent Staph infections = Chédiak-Higashi. The problem isn't the killing chemistry — it's that lysosomes can't fuse properly with phagosomes.

stations.label: Station 10
stations.title: NADPH oxidase generates superoxide
stations.subtitle: The burst ignites.
stations.previously: The bacterium is sealed in the phagolysosome. Granules have fused. Now the neutrophil fires its most destructive weapon — and it starts with a single enzyme that takes oxygen from the air.
stations.tagline: Three enzymes. Each makes a more lethal compound than the last.
stations.story:
The respiratory burst is a rapid spike in oxygen consumption that generates toxic molecules. It runs on an assembly line. This is the most tested pathway in all of innate immunity:

  1. NADPH oxidase makes superoxide (O₂⁻). The first step. Without it, nothing downstream happens.
  2. Superoxide dismutase converts O₂⁻ into hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂).
  3. Myeloperoxidase, the closer, takes H₂O₂ and chloride and makes hypochlorous acid (HOCl). Literal bleach, made inside your neutrophils.

diagrams.caption: The assembly line, with each product more dangerous than the last. The bacterium below is destroyed in step with the cascade — all happening inside the phagolysosome.

flow.text: The bacterium is inside the phagosome. Killing starts now.
flow.subtext: The respiratory burst is about to fire. Oxygen consumption spikes.
flow.insight: Killing chemistry begins inside the phagosome

flow.text: Step 1 · NADPH oxidase → superoxide (O₂⁻).
flow.subtext: Embedded in the phagosome membrane. The first killing molecule. Everything downstream depends on this.
flow.insight: NADPH oxidase makes superoxide (O₂⁻)

flow.text: Step 2 · Superoxide dismutase → H₂O₂.
flow.subtext: SOD converts O₂⁻ into hydrogen peroxide. More reactive. More lethal.
flow.insight: SOD converts O₂⁻ → H₂O₂

flow.prompt: Each enzyme makes a more dangerous compound. What's next — and why the stepwise build?

flow.text: Step 3 · Myeloperoxidase + Cl⁻ → HOCl (bleach).
flow.subtext: MPO is the closer. Literal bleach, made inside your neutrophil.
flow.insight: MPO makes HOCl (bleach) from H₂O₂ + Cl⁻

flow.text: The full assembly line, each step more lethal.
flow.subtext: O₂ → O₂⁻ → H₂O₂ → HOCl. The neutrophil's killing arsenal, end to end.
flow.insight: Pipeline: O₂ → O₂⁻ → H₂O₂ → HOCl

flow.question: Which enzyme makes the first product of the respiratory burst?

feedback.correct: Right. Everything downstream depends on NADPH oxidase. Without it, no superoxide, no H₂O₂, no HOCl. This is exactly the defect in CGD — coming up next.
feedback.wrong: Order matters: NADPH oxidase is first and makes superoxide. SOD and MPO come later. NADPH oxidase is the gate — whole burst depends on it.

flow.text: HOCl destroys the bacterium inside the phagosome.
flow.subtext: Bleach is lethal. The bacterium's membranes, proteins, and DNA get torn apart.
flow.insight: HOCl kills the bacterium → infection resolved

flow.text: This is the most tested pathway in all of innate immunity.
flow.subtext: Break the first enzyme → CGD. Break the last enzyme → MPO deficiency. Coming up.
flow.insight: Two diseases sit on this pipeline: CGD and MPO deficiency

stations.bridge: Bacterial pneumonia. Neutrophils phagocytosing gram-positive diplococci. The respiratory burst is firing — and one enzyme starts the whole cascade.
stations.vignette: A 47-year-old man is hospitalized with acute bacterial pneumonia. Examination of bronchoalveolar lavage fluid shows numerous neutrophils actively phagocytosing gram-positive diplococci.
stations.stem: Which of the following enzymes is most directly responsible for initiating intracellular killing of these organisms within neutrophils?
stations.explanation:
The word that matters is initiating. NADPH oxidase is the very first enzyme in the respiratory burst.

Catalase breaks down H₂O₂ into water — protective, not microbicidal. Cyclooxygenase makes prostaglandins. Lysosomal hydrolases digest the microbe's remains after killing. Superoxide dismutase acts second in the burst.
stations.whatsNext: Three enzymes on an assembly line. Each one is a potential point of failure. We're about to see what happens when the very first enzyme is broken — and an entire class of bacteria takes advantage of the gap.

stations.label: Station 11
stations.title: Catalase-positive organism susceptibility
stations.subtitle: No oxidase, no kill.
stations.previously: The respiratory burst depends on NADPH oxidase to fire first. But what happens when that enzyme is genetically missing? The neutrophil still engulfs the bacterium — it just can't kill it.
stations.tagline: And catalase-positive organisms are the ones that exploit the gap.
stations.story:
Chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) is a genetic defect in NADPH oxidase. Most commonly X-linked. The cellular defect: no NADPH oxidase → no superoxide → no respiratory burst.

A neutrophil with CGD can still do most of its job. It rolls, adheres, migrates, phagocytoses beautifully. But then nothing happens. No ROS. The bacterium is trapped but not killed.

Here's where it gets clinically beautiful. Some bacteria make their own hydrogen peroxide. A healthy-enough neutrophil can hijack that bacterial H₂O₂ to compensate. But this rescue only works if the bacterium makes H₂O₂ and doesn't destroy it.

Catalase-positive organisms produce H₂O₂ and then destroy it. They deny the neutrophil any substrate. In CGD, catalase-positive organisms are lethal.

The classic organisms: Staphylococcus aureus, Serratia marcescens, Burkholderia cepacia, Nocardia, Aspergillus.

Diagnosis: dihydrorhodamine (DHR) flow cytometry or the older nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) test. Treatment: prophylactic TMP-SMX, itraconazole, and — importantly — interferon-γ.

diagrams.caption: Same broken neutrophil. Two different bugs. The bug\u2019s catalase status decides who lives.
All five are catalase-positive — that\u2019s not a coincidence, it\u2019s the unifying mechanism.

flow.text: In CGD, NADPH oxidase is broken.
flow.subtext: The first enzyme of the burst. Without it, no superoxide ever gets made.
flow.insight: CGD defect: NADPH oxidase (X-linked most common)

flow.text: No O₂⁻. No H₂O₂. No HOCl. Nothing.
flow.subtext: The whole assembly line downstream never runs.
flow.insight: Entire ROS cascade fails downstream of NADPH oxidase

flow.text: The rest of the neutrophil works fine. Rolling. Adhesion. Phagocytosis.
flow.subtext: The bug gets caught. It just doesn't get killed.
flow.insight: Neutrophil phagocytoses normally; killing fails

flow.prompt: Some bacteria make their own H₂O₂. Could the neutrophil steal it?

flow.text: Catalase-negative bugs make H₂O₂ and can't destroy it.
flow.subtext: The CGD neutrophil can still hijack that H₂O₂ and run the rest of the burst. These infections are controlled.
flow.insight: Catalase-NEG bugs → neutrophil can steal H₂O₂ → killed

flow.text: Catalase-positive bugs destroy their own H₂O₂.
flow.subtext: No H₂O₂ left to steal. The bug wins. This is the CGD vulnerability.
flow.insight: Catalase-POS bugs → no H₂O₂ to steal → lethal in CGD

flow.question: A boy with CGD has recurrent abscesses. Which organism is he most likely to suffer from?

feedback.correct: Yes — classic CGD organism. S. aureus is catalase-positive; it destroys its own H₂O₂, so the CGD neutrophil has nothing to steal. Also: Serratia, Burkholderia, Nocardia, Aspergillus.
feedback.wrong: Look at the mechanism: CGD patients are vulnerable to CATALASE-POSITIVE organisms. Strep and Enterococcus are catalase-negative and still handled.

flow.text: Five classic organisms. All catalase-positive.
flow.subtext: S. aureus · Serratia · Burkholderia cepacia · Nocardia · Aspergillus.
flow.insight: CGD organisms: S. aureus, Serratia, Burkholderia, Nocardia, Aspergillus

flow.text: Diagnosis: DHR flow cytometry (or NBT test).
flow.subtext: Treatment: TMP-SMX + itraconazole prophylaxis, plus interferon-γ.
flow.insight: Dx: DHR flow · Rx: TMP-SMX, itraconazole, IFN-γ

stations.bridge: A young boy whose history reads like a textbook entry — Staph, Serratia, Aspergillus, recurrent abscesses. The DHR test is abnormal. What ties all his organisms together?
stations.vignette: A 4-year-old boy presents with recurrent pneumonia, skin abscesses, and hepatic abscesses since infancy. Cultures from previous infections have grown Staphylococcus aureus, Serratia marcescens, and Aspergillus species. Neutrophil count and immunoglobulin levels are normal. A dihydrorhodamine (DHR) flow cytometry test is abnormal.
stations.stem: This patient is most susceptible to infection with which type of organism?
stations.explanation:
The abnormal DHR test confirms CGD. Catalase-positive organisms are dangerous because they produce H₂O₂ and destroy it, eliminating the neutrophil's backup strategy.

Encapsulated bacteria predispose to splenic dysfunction. Mycobacterial susceptibility → IFN-γ receptor defects. Anaerobes aren't the CGD signature. Envelope viruses are T-cell territory.
stations.whatsNext:
So that's what happens when the first enzyme of the burst is broken. But what if only the last enzyme fails? The neutrophil still generates superoxide, still makes H₂O₂ — but can't turn any of it into bleach.

The clinical picture is dramatically different. And much milder. Except when Candida gets involved.

bank.label: Bank 01 · Liver Abscesses
bank.vignette: A 5-year-old boy has had recurrent infections since infancy, including pneumonia, lymphadenitis, and liver abscesses. Cultures have repeatedly grown Staphylococcus aureus, Burkholderia cepacia, and Aspergillus species. CBC shows normal neutrophil counts. Serum immunoglobulins are normal.
bank.stem: Which of the following laboratory tests is most likely to confirm this patient's underlying immunodeficiency?
bank.explanation: NBT (older) or DHR (modern) measures the respiratory burst functionally. In CGD, the dye doesn't get reduced — NBT stays yellow instead of turning blue.

bank.label: Bank 02 · Why Catalase-Positive?
bank.vignette: A child with chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) is hospitalized with Aspergillus pneumonia. Researchers explain that CGD patients are particularly vulnerable to organisms that produce the enzyme catalase.
bank.stem: The increased susceptibility to catalase-positive organisms in CGD is best explained by which of the following?

choices.text: Catalase-positive organisms secrete toxins that paralyze neutrophils
Catalase-positive organisms evade engulfment by phagocytes
Catalase-positive organisms destroy the hydrogen peroxide that CGD neutrophils would otherwise use for bactericidal activity
Catalase-positive organisms resist antibodies and complement

bank.explanation: The backup strategy in CGD neutrophils is to use H₂O₂ produced by the engulfed microbe itself. Catalase-positive organisms degrade their own H₂O₂, removing the substrate that would otherwise rescue killing.

stations.label: Station 12
stations.title: Isolated Candida susceptibility, normal NBT
stations.subtitle: No bleach, just Candida.
stations.previously: In CGD, the respiratory burst never starts. But what if everything works up to the very last step — and only the conversion to bleach fails? That's a much subtler disease.
stations.tagline: Usually silent. Except in diabetics. With fungi.
stations.story:
Suppose neutrophil function testing shows that the early steps of the respiratory burst are fine — superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radical all produced normally. But the final product, hypochlorous acid, is missing.

Walk the pathway. NADPH oxidase: working. Superoxide dismutase: working. The only step left is the conversion of H₂O₂ into HOCl. That's myeloperoxidase.

Myeloperoxidase deficiency is the most common inherited phagocyte defect — but it's often silent. Most patients are asymptomatic because the rest of the respiratory burst plus other antimicrobial mechanisms usually compensates. But there's one classic vulnerability: fungal infections, especially Candida, amplified in diabetics.

diagrams.caption: Same assembly line as Stations 9\u201310, but with step 3 broken. H\u2082O\u2082 accumulates; HOCl is never made.
Most common inherited phagocyte defect, but the least common to cause disease \u2014 except in this one specific scenario.

flow.text: The respiratory burst has three enzymes.
flow.subtext: NADPH oxidase → SOD → MPO. Each step hands off to the next.
flow.insight: Burst recap: NADPH oxidase, SOD, MPO

flow.text: MPO is the final step. H₂O₂ + Cl⁻ → HOCl.
flow.subtext: The closer. The bleach-maker. Everything upstream is already done before MPO acts.
flow.insight: MPO = the final step (H₂O₂ → HOCl)

flow.text: In MPO deficiency: only the last step is broken.
flow.subtext: Superoxide still made. H₂O₂ still made. Just no HOCl.
flow.insight: MPO deficiency: pipeline runs up to H₂O₂, no HOCl

flow.prompt: No HOCl — is that a big deal? What else could still kill the bug?

flow.text: Most patients are asymptomatic.
flow.subtext: H₂O₂ itself kills most bacteria. The upstream machinery compensates.
flow.insight: MPO deficiency usually silent — H₂O₂ still kills most bugs

flow.text: The one exception: diabetic patient → Candida.
flow.subtext: Diabetes on its own impairs neutrophil function. Add no HOCl, and Candida becomes the trap.
flow.insight: Exception: diabetics get Candida infections

flow.question: In MPO deficiency, the respiratory burst is intact up to which molecule?

feedback.correct: Yes. NADPH oxidase and SOD still work. H₂O₂ still made — enough to kill most bugs. Only HOCl is missing.
feedback.wrong: MPO is the LAST enzyme. O₂ is consumed normally. H₂O₂ is produced normally. Just HOCl is missing — so the pipeline runs up through H₂O₂ fine.

flow.text: Don't confuse MPO deficiency with CGD.
flow.subtext: CGD = first step broken (no O₂⁻, no H₂O₂, no HOCl). MPO def = only last step (everything except HOCl).
flow.insight: CGD = whole cascade fails. MPO def = only HOCl missing.

flow.text: Silent — except in diabetics.
flow.subtext: Most common inherited phagocyte defect. Usually no clinical consequence. Diabetes is the tip-off to Candida.
flow.insight: Silent mostly · diabetic + Candida = tip-off

stations.bridge: A young woman with type 1 diabetes and recurrent fungal infections. MPO deficiency is confirmed. The rest of her respiratory burst is intact — only the final step is missing.
stations.vignette: A 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus is evaluated for recurrent fungal infections over the past several years. Genetic testing reveals a deficiency of myeloperoxidase in her neutrophils. Other components of the respiratory burst are intact.
stations.stem: Which of the following defects most directly explains her increased susceptibility to infection?
stations.explanationTitle: Answer · E · Inability to produce hydroxy-halide radicals
stations.explanation:
MPO uses H₂O₂ + Cl⁻ to make hypochlorous acid (HOCl) and related halide-containing oxidants. Without MPO, the cell can generate superoxide, H₂O₂, and hydroxyl radical — but can't convert any of it into the final "bleach" step.

Decreased oxygen consumption (A) would be CGD. Defective degranulation (B) is Chédiak-Higashi. Prostaglandins (C) aren't the defect here. Inability to make H₂O₂ (D) would again be CGD-like.
stations.whatsNext:
So far, we've assumed the bacterium is a passive target that the neutrophil can either kill or fail to kill. But bacteria have been evolving alongside immune systems for billions of years. Some have learned to fight back.

One last twist before we leave this chapter.

bank.label: Bank 01 · The Missing Bleach
bank.vignette: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the clinic for evaluation of frequent skin infections and recurrent otitis media. Testing of his neutrophil respiratory burst shows normal production of O₂⁻, H₂O₂, and •OH after exposure to opsonized bacteria. However, generation of HOCl is decreased after stimulation.
bank.stem: Deficiency of which of the following enzymes best explains these findings?
bank.explanation: Walk the pathway. Oxygen → superoxide → H₂O₂ → HOCl. Superoxide, H₂O₂, and hydroxyl radical are all fine. Only the last step — H₂O₂ to HOCl — is broken. That's MPO.

stations.label: Station 13
stations.title: α-toxin phospholipase destroys neutrophils
stations.subtitle: The bacterium strikes back.
stations.previously: We've seen neutrophils fail from within — missing integrins, broken lysosomes, absent enzymes. But what if the neutrophil is working perfectly, and the bacterium has evolved a way to kill it first?
stations.cardTitle: The bacterium brings its own weapon.
stations.tagline: Gas gangrene, alpha toxin, and the limits of a textbook response.
stations.story:
Everything we just walked through assumes the bacterium is a passive target. It's not. Many microbes have evolved strategies to sabotage the neutrophils sent to kill them.

Consider Clostridium perfringens — an anaerobic gram-positive rod that causes gas gangrene (clostridial myonecrosis) after deep dirty wounds. C. perfringens makes alpha toxin, a phospholipase (lecithinase) that chews up the phospholipids in cell membranes.

Alpha toxin destroys muscle tissue — that's why gas gangrene spreads so fast. But it also destroys the neutrophils sent to stop the infection. The toxin punches holes in neutrophil membranes, lysing them before they can phagocytose.

The textbook response works beautifully against a passive microbe. Against one with a specific counter-move, the whole cascade can fail — and the clinical picture tells you which step is being attacked.

diagrams.caption: Same neutrophil, same bug. With alpha toxin, the bug lyses the neutrophil before phagocytosis can begin.

flow.text: Until now, we assumed the bacterium is a passive target.
flow.subtext: It rolls up, gets eaten, gets killed. But that's the textbook case.
flow.insight: Textbook so far: neutrophil attacker, bacterium passive target

flow.text: Reality: bacteria have weapons too.
flow.subtext: Many organisms produce toxins that attack the neutrophil itself.
flow.insight: Bacteria can be active — producing weapons against host

flow.text: Clostridium perfringens makes alpha toxin.
flow.subtext: Anaerobic gram-positive rod. Classic in dirty deep puncture wounds. Causes gas gangrene.
flow.insight: C. perfringens + alpha toxin = gas gangrene

flow.prompt: Alpha toxin is a phospholipase (lecithinase). What would that do?

flow.text: Alpha toxin is a phospholipase — it chews up cell membranes.
flow.subtext: Also called lecithinase. Targets the phospholipid bilayer directly. Punches holes everywhere.
flow.insight: α-toxin = phospholipase/lecithinase → destroys membranes

flow.text: Neutrophils are lysed before they can act.
flow.subtext: Their membranes are phospholipid too. Alpha toxin doesn't discriminate. Host muscle + neutrophils — all destroyed.
flow.insight: Neutrophil membranes destroyed → lysed before phagocytosis

flow.question: What is alpha toxin's molecular target?

feedback.correct: Right. Alpha toxin is a phospholipase (also called lecithinase) — it cleaves phospholipids in the bilayer. Host cells AND neutrophils are destroyed, because both have phospholipid membranes.
feedback.wrong: Alpha toxin is a PHOSPHOLIPASE, not a protein or signaling toxin. Its target is the phospholipid bilayer — the very structure neutrophil membranes are made of.

flow.text: Clinical picture: gas gangrene — fast, necrotic, lethal.
flow.subtext: Deep puncture wound, rapid swelling, crepitus (gas in tissue), necrotic muscle. Surgical debridement is often needed.
flow.insight: Clinical = gas gangrene: crepitus, necrosis, rapid spread

flow.text: The textbook response fails when the bug has a counter-move.
flow.subtext: This is the final twist of innate immunity: the attacker can be attacked back. Welcome to Chapter 2.
flow.insight: Bacteria can disarm neutrophils — innate immunity isn't invincible

stations.bridge: A man with a deep puncture wound to his leg. Rapid swelling, crepitus, gas in the tissue, necrotic muscle. Clostridium perfringens gas gangrene. How does the organism avoid being killed by neutrophils?
stations.vignette: A 34-year-old man with a deep puncture wound to his lower leg develops rapidly progressive pain, swelling, and crepitus at the site. Imaging shows gas within the soft tissues, and surgical exploration reveals necrotic muscle consistent with clostridial myonecrosis. This organism produces a toxin that contributes to tissue destruction and also helps it avoid killing by neutrophils.
stations.stem: Which of the following best describes how Clostridium perfringens evades destruction by neutrophils in patients with gas gangrene?

choices.text: Damaging neutrophil membranes by means of a membrane-damaging toxin
Preventing contact with neutrophils by means of a polysaccharide capsule
Preventing phagocytosis by disruption of neutrophil actin polymerization
Survival in the cytoplasm of a neutrophil
Survival in the phagosome of a macrophage

stations.explanation:
Alpha toxin (lecithinase/phospholipase C) breaks down membrane phospholipids, destroying muscle tissue AND lysing neutrophils before they can phagocytose.

C. perfringens doesn't rely on a prominent capsule (B). It doesn't disrupt actin (C). It doesn't survive inside neutrophils (D) or macrophages (E) — it's an extracellular pathogen that kills quickly.
stations.whatsNext:
You've watched the whole cascade — every step, every failure mode. The bacterium was destroyed (or wasn't). But while you were focused on that single cell, a lot was happening to the tissue around it. The redness. The swelling. The warmth. The throbbing pain.

Those weren't side effects. They were the cost of the fight.

chapters.title: Acute inflammation
chapters.subtitle: Every redness, every swelling, every throb has a molecular cause — and each one follows a specific rule.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: Endothelial contraction → protein-rich exudate
stations.subtitle: Vessels gap open.
stations.tagline: Not magic. Plasma, leaving a vessel it shouldn't have left.
stations.story:
A wasp stings a child on the hand. Within twenty minutes, the area is puffy, warm, and raised.

The venom released a cocktail of inflammatory mediators — histamine, kinins, prostaglandins. These bound receptors on endothelial cells of small venules under the skin and did something specific: they made the endothelial cells contract, pulling apart from their neighbors and opening tiny gaps between cells.

Before the sting, the endothelium was a tight sealed wall. After, it's a leaky one. And once the wall is leaky, plasma escapes from vessels into the interstitial space. That's what edema is. Not magic. Just plasma that used to be inside a vessel and is now outside one.

Vasodilation also happens — that's why the area turns red and warm. But vasodilation alone doesn't move fluid out. To get swelling, you need the wall to leak. And the way the wall leaks, in early acute inflammation, is endothelial gap formation.

diagrams.caption: Same venule, two states. Mediators triggered actin/myosin contraction in endothelial cells \u2014 the wall becomes leaky.

flow.text: Normal vessel: endothelial cells tightly packed, wall is sealed.
flow.subtext: Plasma stays in. Proteins stay in. Nothing leaks.
flow.insight: Normal vessel = sealed, no leak

flow.text: Injury → mediators released (histamine, bradykinin).
flow.subtext: Arterioles vasodilate → redness (rubor) and warmth (calor).
flow.insight: Mediators released → arterioles dilate → rubor + calor

flow.text: Venule endothelium contracts — gaps open between cells.
flow.subtext: Cells don't die. They just pull apart. Actin/myosin contracts → cells retract → gaps.
flow.insight: Postcapillary venule endothelium retracts → gaps

flow.prompt: Endothelial gaps are open — what escapes through them first?

flow.text: Plasma proteins escape through the gaps.
flow.subtext: This is an exudate — protein-rich, high specific gravity. Different from a simple transudate.
flow.insight: Protein-rich exudate escapes into tissue

flow.text: Water follows the protein → tissue edema.
flow.subtext: Osmotic pressure shifts. Plasma oncotic pressure falls, tissue rises. Fluid pours out. SWELLING.
flow.insight: Osmotic drag → edema (tumor)

flow.question: What's the key change at the venule that lets plasma leak out?

choices.text: Endothelial cells die from toxin exposure

feedback.correct: Right. Endothelial contraction is the reversible mechanism of acute inflammation. Cells pull apart, gaps form, wall leaks — then cells relax back.
feedback.wrong: Cells don't die in acute inflammation — that would be necrosis, not reversible. The mechanism is contraction: actin/myosin pulls cells apart, creating reversible gaps.

flow.text: Three cardinal signs so far: rubor, calor, tumor.
flow.subtext: Redness (rubor), warmth (calor), swelling (tumor). The vessel story — visible at the surface.
flow.insight: 3 cardinal signs: rubor, calor, tumor

flow.text: Missing: DOLOR — pain. That's the next station.
flow.subtext: Why does inflamed tissue hurt? There's a specific mediator. Coming up.
flow.insight: 4th cardinal sign = dolor (pain) → next station

stations.bridge: A 7-year-old girl has just been stung by a wasp. Within 20 minutes her hand is visibly swollen. You know what's happening inside her vessels — the question is to name it.
