stations.vignette: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the clinic shortly after being stung by a wasp on her hand. Within 20 minutes, the affected area becomes swollen and warm. The swelling is due to movement of plasma fluid into the interstitial space.
stations.stem: Which of the following processes most directly accounts for this extravascular fluid accumulation?
stations.explanationTitle: Answer · C · Gap formation between endothelial cells
stations.explanation:
Endothelial cells contract in response to mediators like histamine and bradykinin, pulling away from each other and opening gaps. Plasma leaks through those gaps.

Demargination is white cells re-entering circulation. Fibrin thrombi are intravascular clots. Vasoconstriction would reduce blood flow. Vasodilation produces redness and warmth but not fluid escape by itself.
stations.whatsNext:
So now you understand the mechanics of the swelling. But there's a detail the child would insist on if she could talk: the sting doesn't just swell. It throbs. It hurts.

Edema alone doesn't hurt much. Some specific chemical has to be directly stimulating the pain fibers in the skin.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Bradykinin as the pain mediator
stations.subtitle: Bradykinin brings pain.
stations.previously: You know why the tissue swells. But edema alone isn't painful. So which mediator is directly activating the pain nerves?
stations.tagline: Redness, swelling, and pain — all from the same source.
stations.story:
Bradykinin — a small peptide from the kinin cascade — does three things, each mapping onto a cardinal sign of inflammation:

  1. Potent vasodilator → redness and warmth
  2. Increases vascular permeability at postcapillary venules → swelling
  3. Directly stimulates pain fibers → pain

When you feel a sting go from "sharp" to "throbbing," that's bradykinin at work. When an ACE inhibitor gives a patient a dry cough or (rarely) angioedema, that's also bradykinin — because ACE normally degrades it.

Histamine causes rapid vasodilation and permeability too. But histamine doesn't produce much pain. Bradykinin does.

diagrams.caption: Three effects, three cardinal signs. Pain is the discriminator that tells you bradykinin (not histamine).

flow.text: The 4th cardinal sign: DOLOR — pain.
flow.subtext: The wasp sting burns. Cellulitis throbs. A sprained ankle aches. All inflammation hurts.
flow.insight: 4th cardinal sign = dolor (pain)

flow.text: The mediator is bradykinin.
flow.subtext: Histamine makes the vessel leak. Bradykinin adds the ache.
flow.insight: Pain mediator in inflammation = bradykinin

flow.text: Made from kininogen by kallikrein — triggered by Factor XII.
flow.subtext: Hageman factor (Factor XII) activates kallikrein. Kallikrein cleaves kininogen → bradykinin.
flow.insight: Factor XII → kallikrein → bradykinin

flow.prompt: Bradykinin causes pain. What else does it do?

flow.text: Bradykinin also causes vasodilation + permeability.
flow.subtext: Triple-threat mediator: pain + leak + vasodilation. All three cardinal signs in one molecule.
flow.insight: Bradykinin = pain + vasodilation + permeability

flow.text: ACE degrades bradykinin. Block ACE → bradykinin builds up.
flow.subtext: Angiotensin-converting enzyme is the off-switch. ACE inhibitors block that off-switch.
flow.insight: ACE is the bradykinin off-switch

flow.question: A patient on an ACE inhibitor develops a dry cough. What's the mechanism?

choices.text: Histamine release from lung mast cells

feedback.correct: Exactly. ACE normally breaks down bradykinin. Block ACE and bradykinin accumulates in airways → dry cough. Rarely: angioedema.
feedback.wrong: ACE inhibitors DECREASE angiotensin II (that's the point). The cough is from bradykinin buildup — because ACE also degrades bradykinin, and you blocked ACE.

flow.text: ACE inhibitor side effects = bradykinin story.
flow.subtext: Dry cough (very common) and angioedema (rare, dangerous). Same mechanism.
flow.insight: ACEI side effects: dry cough + angioedema (bradykinin buildup)

flow.text: Pain cardinal: throbbing inflammation. Drug cardinal: dry ACE-I cough.
flow.subtext: Same molecule, two stories. Now the whole vessel picture is complete — and we're ready to look upstream at the first-responder mediator.
flow.insight: Bradykinin: the pain story AND the ACE-I cough story

stations.bridge: Same wasp sting, slightly older boy. This time the redness, warmth, swelling, and pain are all being driven by a single mediator at the postcapillary venules.
stations.vignette: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the clinic 20 minutes after being stung by a wasp on his forearm. Within minutes of the sting, the area became red, warm, swollen, and painful. On examination, there is localized erythema and edema surrounding the puncture site.
stations.stem: The observed changes are primarily due to the action of which of the following chemical mediators at postcapillary venules?
stations.explanation:
Pain + swelling + redness at postcapillary venules within minutes = bradykinin.

C3b opsonizes microbes — doesn't produce pain. IL-1 is systemic (fever, acute phase response). Phospholipase C is an intracellular signaling enzyme. Thromboxane A₂ causes vasoconstriction, opposite of what's happening.
stations.whatsNext: Bradykinin is powerful — but it's not actually the first mediator on the scene. Before bradykinin has even been assembled, another chemical has already fired. It's sitting, pre-made, in granules all over your skin and mucosa, ready to release the instant anything goes wrong.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Biphasic permeability — histamine vs late phase
stations.subtitle: Histamine fires first.
stations.previously: Bradykinin does the heavy lifting. But it takes time to assemble. Meanwhile, another mediator is already stored in your skin — ready to fire within seconds of any injury.
stations.cardTitle: The first wave arrives before anything else.
stations.tagline: And it's the part an antihistamine actually blocks.
stations.story:
When tissue is injured — burn, sting, scratch, allergic exposure — mast cells in the area degranulate within seconds. They don't have to wait for synthesis; histamine is preformed, ready in granules.

Histamine binds H1 receptors on endothelial cells in postcapillary venules, causing contraction, gap formation, and permeability. Vessels also dilate. The result is the earliest phase of inflammation — the immediate wheal.

In controlled experiments, vascular permeability shows two distinct peaks:

  • Peak A: immediate, within minutes. Histamine → H1.
  • Peak B: broader, delayed, hours later. Leukotrienes, prostaglandins, cellular infiltration.

An H1 antagonist abolishes Peak A. Peak B is unchanged. That's the whole pharmacology of first-generation antihistamines.

diagrams.caption: An H\u2081 blocker abolishes the histamine-driven Peak A. Peak B is unchanged because it runs on entirely different mediators.

flow.text: Inflammation needs a first responder — fast.
flow.subtext: Bradykinin takes time to synthesize. Prostaglandins take time to synthesize. Something else goes first.
flow.insight: Inflammation needs an instant first mediator

flow.text: Histamine — preformed in granules, ready to release.
flow.subtext: No synthesis needed. Open the granule, histamine pours out. Instant response.
flow.insight: Histamine = preformed → instant release

flow.text: Stored in mast cells (tissue) + basophils (blood).
flow.subtext: Mast cells sit IN the tissue, waiting. Basophils circulate. Both degranulate on demand.
flow.insight: Sources: mast cells (tissue), basophils (blood)

flow.prompt: What triggers the mast cell to degranulate?

flow.text: Triggers: IgE crosslinking, trauma, C3a/C5a, drugs, cold.
flow.subtext: Multiple triggers, one response: granules open, histamine floods out.
flow.insight: Triggers: IgE, trauma, complement (C3a/C5a), drugs, temperature

flow.text: H1 receptor = the inflammation story.
flow.subtext: Vasodilation + increased permeability. This is what antihistamines block.
flow.insight: H1 → vasodilation + permeability (the leak)

flow.question: Why is histamine the FIRST mediator to act in inflammation?

choices.text: It's preformed in granules — no synthesis needed
It has the highest plasma concentration

feedback.correct: Right. Everything else (PGs, LTs, bradykinin) must be synthesized — takes minutes to hours. Histamine is already made, just release it.
feedback.wrong: Speed of release is the key — histamine is pre-made in granules, so it's released instantly. Other mediators need to be synthesized before they can act.

flow.text: H2 receptor = totally different job (gastric acid).
flow.subtext: Same amine, different receptor. H2 on parietal cells → HCl. H2 blockers (ranitidine-class) treat GERD.
flow.insight: H2 → gastric acid secretion (not inflammation)

flow.text: Histamine finishes the vessel story. Next: the cells.
flow.subtext: The vessel's job is now done — it's leaky, painful, swollen. What comes through the wall next is the cellular flood.
flow.insight: Vessel story complete → next: cells pour out

stations.bridge: Back to the lab. A controlled thermal injury, tracer dye in the bloodstream, two peaks of vascular permeability — one within minutes, one hours later. An H1 blocker is given.
stations.vignette: In a laboratory study, a small area of skin is exposed to a brief thermal stimulus (54°C for 20 seconds). After intravenous injection of a tracer dye, investigators observe two phases of increased vascular permeability: an immediate sharp rise within minutes (Peak A) followed by a delayed, broader increase several hours later (Peak B).
stations.stem: Pretreatment with an H1-receptor antagonist would most significantly reduce dye extravasation at which of the following labeled peaks?
stations.explanation:
Peak A is strongly histamine-dependent via H1 receptors on venular endothelium. Block H1 and the immediate peak drops dramatically.

Peak B is driven by other mediators — newly synthesized leukotrienes and prostaglandins, plus infiltrating inflammatory cells. H1 blockade doesn't meaningfully affect those.
stations.whatsNext:
So the mediators are firing. Histamine first, then bradykinin, then the later stuff. But these chemical signals don't act in a vacuum — their job is to get cells to the tissue.

And not one cell at a time. In a real infection, thousands of neutrophils pour out of the vessels simultaneously. How does the body coordinate that?

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Chemokine gradient drives PMN migration
stations.subtitle: Thousands pour out.
stations.previously: Chapter 1 traced a single neutrophil through the steps of exiting a vessel. But acute inflammation isn't one cell at a time — it's a coordinated wave. How does the body make that happen?
stations.tagline: That's how cellulitis goes from nothing to full involvement in hours.
stations.story:
In Chapter 1 we traced a single neutrophil. In real cellulitis, this happens to thousands of cells at once, in a coordinated wave.

What makes that coordination possible? Altered expression of adhesion molecules — on both sides of the vessel wall simultaneously:

  • Endothelium upregulates selectins (rolling)
  • Endothelium upregulates ICAM-1 and VCAM-1 (firm adhesion)
  • Neutrophil integrins shift to high-affinity state
  • PECAM-1 at endothelial junctions enables transmigration

Every one of these is driven by cytokines pouring out of the infected tissue — IL-1, TNF, IL-8.

Phagocytic ability, lysosomal enzymes, Fc receptors — these matter for what a neutrophil does after it reaches tissue. They don't explain how it got there. The rate-limiting step for neutrophil accumulation is adhesion and migration.

diagrams.caption: All four molecule pairs upregulate together. That\u2019s why thousands of cells can cross the wall on the same timescale.

flow.text: Hours into infection → the body needs more neutrophils.
flow.subtext: A few thousand PMNs won't hold a serious bug. Reinforcements are ordered.
flow.insight: Sustained infection → mobilize reserve pools

flow.text: Step 1 — Demargination.
flow.subtext: Marginated neutrophils lining vessel walls rejoin the flow. Instant boost.
flow.insight: Demargination: wall-adherent PMNs rejoin circulation

flow.text: Step 2 — Bone marrow releases the storage pool.
flow.subtext: The marrow has a reserve of mature neutrophils waiting. On cytokine signals (G-CSF, IL-1, TNF), they're pushed into blood.
flow.insight: Marrow storage pool released → leukocytosis

flow.prompt: The bone marrow is pushing hard. What shows up in blood that normally wouldn't?

flow.text: Immature neutrophils escape into blood — bands.
flow.subtext: Marrow can't keep up with demand. Immature forms get released early. Bands have a U-shaped (unsegmented) nucleus.
flow.insight: Bands = immature neutrophils (U-nucleus)

flow.text: This pattern has a name: LEFT SHIFT.
flow.subtext: More bands (less mature) than usual. Think of a maturation timeline pushed to the LEFT (less-mature end).
flow.insight: Left shift = ↑ bands, sign of bacterial infection

flow.question: On a CBC, what defines a "left shift"?

choices.text: Increased immature neutrophils (bands) relative to mature
Decreased total white blood cell count

feedback.correct: Yes. Left shift = marrow pushing out cells before they fully mature. Typical of bacterial infection and other strong acute stressors.
feedback.wrong: Left shift is specifically about MATURITY, not count or lineage. It means more immature neutrophils (bands) are showing up in blood.

flow.text: CBC fingerprint: WBC ↑, neutrophils ↑, bands ↑.
flow.subtext: Classic bacterial infection pattern. Viral infections look different (lymphocytosis, often). Know this fingerprint.
flow.insight: CBC: WBC ↑ + neutrophils ↑ + bands ↑ = bacterial

flow.text: Next: what happens to the lymph node downstream?
flow.subtext: The infection produces a traceable path. Drainage goes somewhere. The node responds.
flow.insight: Next station: lymph node warning (lymphadenitis)

stations.bridge: Cellulitis of the leg. Thousands of neutrophils accumulating within hours. You know the rate-limiting step for all of them — pick it out of the distractors.
stations.vignette: A 47-year-old woman presents with acute cellulitis of the lower leg. Within hours of infection, neutrophils accumulate rapidly at the site of inflammation. This process requires neutrophils to leave the bloodstream by passing between endothelial cells of postcapillary venules.
stations.stem: Which of the following changes most directly accounts for the increased migration of neutrophils into the surrounding tissue?
stations.explanationTitle: Answer · A · Altered expression of adhesion molecules
stations.explanation:
The rate-limiting step for neutrophil extravasation is the coordinated expression of adhesion molecules: selectins, ICAM-1, and integrins activated to high-affinity state.

Phagocytic ability (B) matters once the cell is in tissue. Lysosomal enzymes (C) help kill microbes after engulfment. IL-1 (D) is upstream — it's what triggers adhesion molecule expression. Fc receptors (E) bind opsonized microbes.
stations.whatsNext:
The neutrophils are in the tissue. But some bacteria and antigens don't stay local — they drain along lymphatic channels to nearby lymph nodes. And when they arrive, the nodes themselves start to swell.

Sometimes that swelling tells you something important about where the infection came from.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Reactive lymphadenopathy — the draining node
stations.subtitle: The groin swells.
stations.previously: Infection in the foot doesn't stay in the foot. It travels along lymphatic channels. And where those channels end, something very specific happens — on the same side of the body as the original infection.
stations.cardTitle: The groin swells — but the infection is in the foot.
stations.tagline: Lymph drains on the side it came from. Always.
stations.story:
The lymphatic system is the body's drainage network. Every tissue has lymphatic vessels collecting interstitial fluid — and anything in it (bacteria, antigens, debris) — and carrying it toward the bloodstream. Before getting to the blood, the lymph passes through lymph nodes: checkpoints where immune cells screen the material.

When a peripheral tissue gets infected, some bacteria and antigens drain to nearby nodes. The nodes respond by ramping up immune activity. They get bigger. They get tender. This is reactive lymphadenopathy — not a sign of something gone wrong, but of the immune system working.

The foot drains to popliteal nodes, then up the leg to the inguinal (groin) nodes on the same side.

Lymph drains on the side of the body it came from. A hand infection enlarges axillary nodes on the same side. Scalp → cervical. Breast → ipsilateral axilla.

diagrams.caption: Lymph always drains to the ipsilateral node. The node that swells tells you which territory is infected \u2014 and on which side.

flow.text: Local inflammation → fluid drains from tissue into afferent lymphatics.
flow.subtext: Exudate doesn't just stay in tissue. It has to go somewhere — into lymphatic channels.
flow.insight: Inflamed tissue drains into afferent lymphatics

flow.text: Lymphatics deliver the bug's antigens + cytokines to the regional node.
flow.subtext: Every tissue has a predictable drainage territory. Foot → inguinal. Hand → axillary. Scalp → cervical.
flow.insight: Each territory drains to a specific regional node

flow.prompt: The node receives antigen + cytokines. What happens inside?

flow.text: Immune cells in the node proliferate. The node enlarges.
flow.subtext: B cells multiply in germinal centers. T cells and macrophages expand. The node grows.
flow.insight: Node enlargement = lymphadenopathy

flow.text: Clinically: tender, warm, movable node = lymphadenitis.
flow.subtext: Inflammation of the node itself. Different from simple lymphadenopathy (which just means big, non-specifically).
flow.insight: Lymphadenitis = inflamed node (tender, warm)

flow.text: If the lymphatic vessel itself inflames → red streaks.
flow.subtext: That's lymphangitis. Visible lines of inflammation tracking from the wound up toward the node.
flow.insight: Lymphangitis = red streaks = infected vessel (emergency)

flow.question: What's the difference between lymphadenitis and lymphangitis?

choices.text: Adenitis = inflamed NODE; angitis = inflamed VESSEL (red streaks)
Adenitis is infectious; angitis is autoimmune

feedback.correct: Right. The roots: "adeno" = gland/node, "angio" = vessel. Swollen node = lymphadenitis. Red streaks visible on skin = lymphangitis (the vessel itself is inflamed).
feedback.wrong: They describe different structures: ADENitis = inflamed NODE, angITIS = inflamed VESSEL. The distinction matters clinically — red streaks warn of rapid spread.

flow.text: Red streaks on skin = medical urgency.
flow.subtext: Suggests the infection is spreading fast up the lymphatic channel. Risk: bacteremia, sepsis. Treat aggressively.
flow.insight: Red streaks = urgent — needs prompt antibiotics

flow.text: The body is beating the infection — but collateral damage is happening.
flow.subtext: Neutrophils are potent killers. They don't aim carefully. Host tissue pays.
flow.insight: Next station: friendly fire — host tissue damage

stations.bridge: A diabetic man with a 10-day foot ulcer. His groin — on the same side — is swelling and tender. Five possible mechanisms are on the table. Only one fits the anatomy.
stations.vignette: A 50-year-old man with poorly controlled diabetes presents with a painful ulcer on the plantar surface of his left foot that began after stepping on a nail 10 days ago. Over the past several days, he has noticed progressive swelling and tenderness in his left groin. Physical examination shows an erythematous, draining wound on the sole of the foot with purulent material and an enlarged, tender inguinal mass on the same side.
stations.stem: Which of the following is the most likely cause of the groin swelling?

choices.text: Localized edema from a protein-deficient diet
Obstruction of lymphatic drainage in vessels near the knee
Obstruction of venous return from the right leg
Recirculation of neutrophils from the wound site

stations.explanation:
The infected foot drains lymph toward the inguinal nodes on the same side. Inflammation + bacterial/antigen delivery enlarges the nodes.

Angiogenesis (A) is tissue repair, not lymphadenopathy. Protein-deficient edema (B) would be generalized. Right-leg venous obstruction (D) is wrong side. Neutrophil recirculation (E) doesn't cause lymph node mass formation.
stations.whatsNext:
So far, the inflammatory response looks heroic. Cytokines, cells, chemicals — all aimed at the invader. But there's a dark side to this biology that we need to confront.

The weapons your immune system uses against bacteria don't only hit bacteria. They also hit you.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: ROS-mediated bystander tissue injury
stations.subtitle: Friendly fire.
stations.previously: Reactive oxygen species are the weapon. Inside a phagolysosome, they're precise. But when millions of neutrophils are flooding a tissue, those chemicals don't stay contained.
stations.cardTitle: The weapon hits the patient too.
stations.tagline: A core mechanism of tissue damage in pneumonia, ARDS, and reperfusion injury.
stations.story:
In Chapter 1 we celebrated the respiratory burst. Reactive oxygen species — superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, hypochlorous acid — kill bacteria inside phagolysosomes. Precise and powerful.

But chemicals don't stay in neat compartments. When a neutrophil dies or is heavily activated, ROS leak out. When a tissue is flooded with neutrophils — a pneumonia with millions of activated cells — a great deal of ROS spills into the extracellular space.

And those ROS don't care whether they're hitting bacterial proteins or your own lung tissue.

In bacterial pneumonia, neutrophil ROS damage alveolar epithelium and capillary endothelium, contributing to the pulmonary edema and impaired gas exchange. In ARDS, this becomes life-threatening. In atherosclerosis, chronic oxidative damage drives vascular disease. In reperfusion injury, a sudden burst of ROS destroys already-stressed tissue.

The immune system isn't an unalloyed good. Every weapon it uses against microbes can also harm you.

diagrams.caption: Same chemistry, different address. Contained ROS kills bacteria. Extracellular ROS kills bacteria and tissue.

flow.text: Neutrophils release killing enzymes into tissue.
flow.subtext: Elastase, collagenase, MMPs. Intended for bacteria. Good at destroying proteins.
flow.insight: Neutrophils release proteases into surrounding tissue

flow.text: Elastase degrades elastin.
flow.subtext: Elastin is a connective tissue protein — gives stretch/recoil. Abundant in lungs, skin, arteries.
flow.insight: Elastase cleaves elastin → loss of tissue recoil

flow.text: Problem: host tissue is also elastin.
flow.subtext: Elastase doesn't distinguish bug protein from your protein. Lung alveoli, skin, arteries — all at risk.
flow.insight: Elastase attacks host tissue too — collateral damage

flow.prompt: The body has to protect its own tissue. What stops elastase?

flow.text: Alpha-1 antitrypsin (A1AT) inhibits elastase.
flow.subtext: A serpin (SERine Protease INhibitor). Made by the liver. Travels in blood. Binds elastase, shuts it down.
flow.insight: A1AT = serpin that inhibits elastase

flow.text: A1AT deficiency → unchecked elastase.
flow.subtext: No brake. Elastase chews through elastin everywhere it finds it. Two classic organs fail: LUNGS and LIVER.
flow.insight: A1AT deficiency → unchecked elastase destruction

flow.question: Classic A1AT deficiency presentation?

choices.text: Young adult, panacinar emphysema (lower lobes) + liver disease
Elderly smoker with centrilobular emphysema (upper lobes)
Child with recurrent sinusitis and situs inversus

feedback.correct: Right. A1AT deficiency emphysema is PANACINAR (whole acinus), LOWER lobes, and can occur in YOUNG nonsmokers. Plus liver disease (misfolded A1AT accumulates there).
feedback.wrong: The second answer is classic COPD/smoker emphysema — centrilobular, upper lobes, older. The third is Kartagener. A1AT: young patient + lower lobe emphysema + liver disease.

flow.text: Lungs: panacinar emphysema, lower lobes, in young adults.
flow.subtext: Loss of alveolar elastin destroys recoil. Emphysema hits lower lobes (opposite of smoker emphysema, which is upper). Can appear in nonsmokers.
flow.insight: A1AT lung: panacinar, lower lobes, young

flow.text: Liver: misfolded A1AT protein accumulates → cirrhosis.
flow.subtext: The defective A1AT can't leave hepatocytes. It builds up as PAS-positive globules inside liver cells. Damage + fibrosis + cirrhosis.
flow.insight: Liver: misfolded A1AT traps in hepatocytes → cirrhosis

stations.bridge: An older man with lobar pneumonia. His neutrophils are killing pneumococci inside phagolysosomes — but the same chemistry is also eating into his lung parenchyma. Six possible mediators; one is doing the collateral damage.
stations.vignette: A 60-year-old man is hospitalized for community-acquired pneumonia with fever, productive cough, and lobar consolidation on chest imaging. Neutrophils infiltrate the infected lung tissue and begin killing bacteria within phagolysosomes. During this process, collateral damage to surrounding lung tissue occurs.
stations.stem: Which of the following mediators released by inflammatory cells is most directly responsible for this unintended tissue injury?
stations.explanation:
Neutrophil ROS are highly reactive and don't distinguish bacterial from host molecules. They leak from dying/activated neutrophils and injure nearby host tissue.

Complement (A) contributes to inflammation but isn't the primary direct cause of oxidative injury. Defensins (B) focus on microbes. IL-1 (C) drives systemic inflammation. Prostaglandins (D) contribute to pain and fever. TGF-β (F) is regulatory/fibrotic.
stations.whatsNext:
The infection gets contained. The neutrophils have done their work — fought, killed, died. The tissue is a mess: dead cells, debris, fibrin, dead neutrophils.

Who cleans it all up? And when? The answer involves a completely different cell, taking over at a predictable point in the timeline.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Macrophage resolution and efferocytosis
stations.subtitle: Macrophages finish.
stations.previously: After the bacteria are killed, the tissue is full of dead cells and debris. Neutrophils can't clean that up — they're part of the mess. Something else has to take over.
stations.tagline: By day 5, they're the dominant cell in a healing wound.
stations.story:
An abscess on the upper thigh is drained. Thick yellow-green pus pours out. Over the next several days, redness fades, tenderness decreases, the hole closes.

Five days later, you biopsy the healing tissue. What cells are there?

Not neutrophils. The neutrophil era is short. Neutrophils arrive within hours of infection, kill and die in huge numbers, and most are gone within days. The pus you drained? Largely dead neutrophils.

Once infection is contained, macrophages dominate the next phase. They do three things:

  1. Cleanup: phagocytose dead cells, debris, fibrin, dead neutrophils, bacterial fragments
  2. Signaling: release cytokines and growth factors that recruit fibroblasts and stimulate angiogenesis
  3. Repair coordination: orchestrate transition from inflammation to healing

Days 1–2: mostly neutrophils. Days 3–5: mix, macrophages becoming dominant. Days 5+: mostly macrophages and fibroblasts. If a question asks what cells are present at a particular time after an insult, think about where you are in this timeline.

diagrams.caption: Three cell lines, one timeline. Day 5 sits squarely in the macrophage-dominant zone \u2014 locate yourself on the curve, and the answer follows.

flow.text: Days into infection — tissue is a battlefield.
flow.subtext: Dead bacteria, lysed neutrophils, cellular debris, fibrin, pus. Something has to clean this up.
flow.insight: After the fight, tissue needs cleanup + repair

flow.text: Macrophages arrive — days 2 to 3.
flow.subtext: Derived from blood monocytes. Bigger, slower, more specialized than neutrophils. They're the second wave.
flow.insight: Macrophages = 2nd wave, days 2-3, from monocytes

flow.text: They phagocytose everything: bacteria remnants, apoptotic neutrophils, debris.
flow.subtext: Macrophages eat bugs AND spent neutrophils. Efferocytosis = eating dying cells. Tissue gets physically cleaned.
flow.insight: Macrophages eat bugs + apoptotic PMNs (efferocytosis)

flow.prompt: How do macrophages RESOLVE (turn off) inflammation?

flow.text: Release anti-inflammatory cytokines: IL-10 and TGF-β.
flow.subtext: IL-10 suppresses IL-1, TNF, IL-6. TGF-β dampens immune activity and promotes healing.
flow.insight: IL-10 + TGF-β = anti-inflammatory resolution signal

flow.text: They also release PDGF, FGF, TGF-β → stimulate fibroblasts.
flow.subtext: Fibroblasts lay down collagen. Granulation tissue forms. Wound heals — often with scar.
flow.insight: PDGF, FGF, TGF-β → fibroblast activation → scar

flow.question: An abscess is drained. Five days later, which cell TYPE dominates the healing tissue?

feedback.correct: Right. Days 1-2 = neutrophils. Days 2-3 = mix. Day 5+ = macrophages dominant, working alongside fibroblasts. Day 5 is firmly in the macrophage phase.
feedback.wrong: Neutrophils dominate the FIRST 1-2 days. By day 5, most neutrophils are gone (apoptosed or cleared). Macrophages take over for resolution and repair.

flow.text: Cell timeline: PMN → Mix → Mac + Fibroblast.
flow.subtext: Days 1-2 neutrophils. Days 3-5 mix. Days 5+ macrophages + fibroblasts. This is high-yield — know the curves.
flow.insight: Timeline: PMN (d1-2) → Mix (d3-5) → Mac/Fibroblast (d5+)

flow.text: Inflammation resolves. Chapter 2 closes.
flow.subtext: Tissue-level story: vessels leaked, cells flooded, collateral damage happened, cleanup crew arrived. Next: the barrier story.
flow.insight: Ch2 complete — vessel → cells → damage → repair

stations.bridge: A drained thigh abscess, five days later. The redness is fading, the wound is closing. Which cell type dominates the healing tissue now?
stations.vignette: A 19-year-old woman presents with a painful, erythematous, fluctuant mass on her upper thigh that began as a small pustule several days ago. Incision and drainage release thick yellow-green purulent material. Over the next several days, the surrounding redness and tenderness gradually improve.
stations.stem: Which of the following cell types is most responsible for the tissue changes present in the lesion approximately 5 days after drainage?
stations.explanation:
By day 5, the acute neutrophil-driven phase is over and macrophages dominate — cleaning up debris, recruiting fibroblasts, and promoting tissue repair.

Eosinophils (A) are for parasites and allergy. Lymphocytes (B) dominate chronic inflammation. Neutrophils (D) dominate days 1–2. Plasma cells (E) make antibodies.
stations.whatsNext:
The local fight is over. The tissue is healing. The cascade worked.

But all of this was local defense — one tissue, one infection. What protects you systemically? What catches bacteria circulating in the bloodstream, organisms slipping past the local response?

That's a completely different story. And it involves an organ you probably never think about.

chapters.title: Complement, spleen, and the encapsulated organisms
chapters.subtitle: The spleen, the neutrophil supply, the flora on your own skin — systemic defenses you never notice until they're gone.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: Nasal colonization as the bacteremia source
stations.subtitle: S. aureus lives there.
stations.previously: You've seen local defense: a cascade of cells, signals, and enzymes that handles a bacterium at the tissue level. But local defense isn't everything. The next chapter is about defenses that work silently, for your whole life, across the whole body.
stations.cardTitle: The bacteria are already on you.
stations.tagline: About 30% of healthy adults carry S. aureus in their nostrils — quietly.
stations.story:
Most of what keeps you alive is invisible. Your skin as a hostile chemical barrier. Trillions of commensal bacteria kept in check by flora and local defenses. Your spleen sitting quietly in your left upper quadrant, doing its work for decades without notice.

Let's start with the most counterintuitive fact in systemic infection. An infection isn't always a germ invading from outside. It can be your own flora taking advantage of a new opportunity.

About 30% of healthy adults are persistent nasal carriers of Staphylococcus aureus. These people aren't sick. Their noses look completely normal. The bacteria just live there — part of the normal skin/mucosal flora, kept in check by local barriers and commensal competitors.

But give S. aureus a way into the bloodstream — an IV line, a surgical drain, a breach in the skin or mucosa — and it translocates. From the nose into the blood, then anywhere. A prosthetic joint is a particularly good landing site (bacteria love biofilms on foreign material), but the same mechanism can seed heart valves, vertebrae, paraspinal spaces.

This is why many hospitals now screen high-risk surgical patients for nasal S. aureus carriage and decolonize with intranasal mupirocin. The nose is the reservoir. Eliminate the reservoir, reduce the bacteremia.

diagrams.caption: The journey from the anterior nares to the hip prosthesis. The surgical wound was clean \u2014 the source was the patient\u2019s own nose all along.

flow.text: About 30% of healthy people carry S. aureus in their nose.
flow.subtext: Anterior nares. Warm, moist, humid. Perfect real estate for a gram-positive coccus.
flow.insight: ≈ 1 in 3 people are nasal S. aureus carriers

flow.text: The nose is a reservoir, not just a transient exposure.
flow.subtext: Persistent colonization. Same strain can live there for months or years.
flow.insight: Nose = persistent reservoir, not transient flora

flow.text: At surgery, bacteria travel: skin, hands, airborne.
flow.subtext: Hands touch face, then glove, then wound. Aerosol lands on prosthesis. The reservoir finds the door.
flow.insight: Self-inoculation — patient's own nose → surgical site

flow.prompt: The S. aureus reached the wound. Why does a hip prosthesis make the infection harder to treat?

flow.text: Prosthetic material lets bacteria form biofilm.
flow.subtext: Bacteria adhere to plastic/metal, secrete polysaccharide matrix, hide from antibiotics and immune cells.
flow.insight: Biofilm on prosthesis → antibiotic resistance + persistence

flow.text: Prevent it upstream: decolonize the nose pre-op.
flow.subtext: Intranasal mupirocin for 5 days before high-risk surgery. Eliminate the reservoir before the operation.
flow.insight: Mupirocin = anti-staph nasal ointment for decolonization

flow.question: A post-op S. aureus bacteremia with no apparent source — what's the single most likely reservoir?

feedback.correct: Exactly. About 30% of people are nasal S. aureus carriers. Self-inoculation from nose to surgical site is far more common than environmental contamination.
feedback.wrong: OR environment and instruments are heavily controlled. The most common source of post-op S. aureus — by a wide margin — is the patient's own nasal flora.

flow.text: Post-op bacteremia + clean wound + no UTI + no pneumonia → check the nose.
flow.subtext: Don't just swab the wound. The reservoir is elsewhere.
flow.insight: Unexplained post-op staph → assume nasal source

flow.text: Next station: the spleen.
flow.subtext: From colonization to bacteremia. And from bacteremia to — the filter. What happens when blood passes through an organ designed to catch bugs?
flow.insight: Next: spleen filtering + encapsulated organisms

stations.bridge: A woman five days after a hip replacement — fever, but a clean-looking incision, no urinary symptoms, no cough. Blood cultures grow S. aureus. The surgical wound isn't the source. So where is it?
stations.vignette: A 60-year-old woman undergoes an uncomplicated total hip arthroplasty. On postoperative day 5, she develops fever and malaise. The surgical incision appears clean and dry without erythema or drainage. She has no cough, dysuria, or abdominal tenderness. Blood cultures are positive for Staphylococcus aureus.
stations.stem: The source of this patient's bacteremia is most likely colonization at which of the following sites?
stations.explanation:
The anterior nares are the primary reservoir for S. aureus in humans — roughly 30% of healthy adults are persistent carriers. In the perioperative setting, bacteria from the nasal reservoir can seed hardware and cause prosthetic joint infection even when the surgical site itself looks clean.

GI tract (B) harbors E. coli and enterococci, not S. aureus. Oropharynx (C) carries S. pneumoniae and viridans streptococci. Perineum (D) and urinary tract (E) would suggest gram-negative or enterococcal bacteremia.
stations.whatsNext: The nose was the source. Now we need to understand what organ should have caught these bacteria before they reached the hip — and what happens to patients who've lost that organ.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Encapsulated-organism clearance requires the spleen
stations.subtitle: Encapsulated killers.
stations.previously: You just saw that normal flora can become a blood-borne pathogen. What normally catches those organisms in the blood? The spleen.
stations.cardTitle: An organ most people never think about.
stations.tagline: Lose it, and a particular kind of bacterium becomes potentially lethal.
stations.story:
The spleen isn't glamorous. Not the thymus, where T cells mature. Not the bone marrow, where everything is born. Just a soft purple organ in the left upper quadrant. Ask a layperson what it does and they'll shrug.

Here's what it does: it filters your blood.

Specifically, the spleen is the one place in the body dedicated to catching pathogens and opsonized particles (things coated with antibody or complement) circulating in the bloodstream. RBCs, pathogens, and debris pass through slow, tortuous sinusoidal channels lined with macrophages — and anything abnormal gets caught and eaten.

The spleen is especially important for encapsulated bacteria. These are organisms wrapped in a polysaccharide capsule — a slick, sugary coat that prevents phagocytes from getting a grip:

  • Streptococcus pneumoniae
  • Haemophilus influenzae type b
  • Neisseria meningitidis
  • Salmonella typhi
  • Klebsiella pneumoniae

Their capsules make them hard to phagocytose unless first coated with antibody or complement — which is exactly what the spleen is specialized to do. It also houses IgM memory B cells that produce early protective antibodies against these organisms.

Remove the spleen, and both functions vanish. The mechanical filter is gone. The dedicated antibody factory is gone. Of all the encapsulated organisms, Streptococcus pneumoniae is by far the most dangerous in asplenic patients.

diagrams.caption: Top: why capsules block phagocytosis, and how opsonization (antibody + complement) restores the grip. Middle: the five encapsulated organisms. Bottom: what asplenic patients lose.

flow.text: The spleen filters blood for bacteria — especially encapsulated ones.
flow.subtext: Blood passes through splenic sinusoids. Macrophages pluck out bacteria + damaged cells.
flow.insight: Spleen = blood filter + antibody factory

flow.text: Capsule = a sugar cloak that hides PAMPs.
flow.subtext: Phagocytes can't grip the slippery surface. Capsule shields the bug from direct killing.
flow.insight: Capsule blocks phagocytosis — slippery to PMNs

flow.text: Solution: opsonize the capsule with antibody + complement.
flow.subtext: Antibody (IgM/IgG) binds capsule. C3b deposits on surface. Both create handles for phagocytes.
flow.insight: Opsonization = handles on capsule (Ab + C3b)

flow.prompt: Which two splenic functions are critical for handling encapsulated organisms?

flow.text: The spleen does two key things.
flow.subtext: 1) Mechanical filter: bacteria trapped in red pulp, eaten by macrophages. 2) IgM memory B cells: specialized antibody production against encapsulated bugs.
flow.insight: Spleen: (1) filter + (2) IgM memory B cells

flow.text: The 5 key encapsulated organisms.
flow.subtext: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, Klebsiella, Group B Strep. All wear a sugar cape. All need the spleen.
flow.insight: 5 capsule bugs: S.pneumo / H.flu / N.mening / Klebs / GBS

flow.question: After splenectomy, which SINGLE organism is most important to vaccinate against?

feedback.correct: Correct. S. pneumoniae is by far the most common and dangerous organism in asplenic patients. Both PCV13 and PPSV23 are given. H. flu and meningococcal vaccines also indicated.
feedback.wrong: S. aureus and E. coli are common pathogens generally, but neither is the top asplenic threat. The capsule + respiratory transmission of S. pneumoniae makes it the #1 danger.

flow.text: Remove the spleen → both functions vanish.
flow.subtext: No mechanical filter. No IgM memory antibody factory. Encapsulated bugs now have free run.
flow.insight: Splenectomy → both filter & antibody loss

flow.text: Next stop: the smear.
flow.subtext: How can we tell, from a simple blood smear, whether the spleen is working? Howell-Jolly bodies — next station.
flow.insight: Next: blood-smear tell of missing/failing spleen

stations.bridge: A clinical scenario about a patient whose spleen has been removed: what class of organism is now most dangerous?
stations.vignette: A 34-year-old man underwent splenectomy two years ago following a motor vehicle collision that caused splenic rupture. His medical team wants to ensure appropriate long-term prophylaxis and vaccination.
stations.stem: This patient is at greatest risk of overwhelming infection from bacteria with which of the following structural features?
stations.explanation:
The spleen is the primary site for clearing encapsulated organisms from the bloodstream. Asplenic patients are especially vulnerable to S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae type b, and N. meningitidis — all encapsulated. Vaccination against all three is mandatory post-splenectomy.

Acid-fast cell walls (A) define mycobacteria, handled by cell-mediated immunity. Flagella (B) don't confer phagocytic resistance. IgA protease (C) is a virulence factor but not the main reason asplenic patients are at risk. LPS (D) is on gram-negative bacteria, which are handled mostly by neutrophils.
stations.whatsNext: If you can't physically examine the spleen, is there a way to see its function on a routine blood test? Yes — and it's elegant.

bank.label: Bonus · Most dangerous organism
bank.vignette: A 40-year-old woman status post splenectomy presents with sudden fever, chills, and hypotension. Blood cultures are pending.
bank.stem: Which organism is most likely responsible for her presentation?
bank.explanation: S. pneumoniae is by far the most common organism in overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI). It causes the majority of fatal cases.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Terminal complement (C5–C9) and Neisseria
stations.subtitle: Three triggers. One kill switch.
stations.previously: The spleen filters encapsulated bacteria from the blood using opsonization. But where do those opsonins come from? Much of the tagging work is done by a parallel defense system — not cells, not antibodies, but a cascade of plasma proteins that can coat a microbe, recruit neutrophils, and even drill a pore through the microbe's membrane on their own.
stations.cardTitle: A kill switch that needs no cell.
stations.tagline: Proteins that tag, opsonize, lyse — all without leaving the plasma.
stations.story:
Complement is a system of about 30 plasma proteins that circulates in an inactive form at all times. When a microbe appears, the proteins activate in a cascading sequence, each one cleaving the next — and the final product is a pore drilled straight through the microbe's membrane.

There are three pathways that can start the cascade. The classical pathway is triggered by an antibody (IgG or IgM) already bound to a microbial surface. The lectin pathway is triggered by mannose-binding lectin (MBL) grabbing mannose sugars on bacterial or fungal surfaces. The alternative pathway is spontaneous — C3 slowly auto-hydrolyzes in plasma and becomes amplified on any microbial surface nearby, no antibody required.

All three pathways converge on one enzyme: C3 convertase. This enzyme cleaves C3 into two fragments. C3a floats away as an anaphylatoxin. C3b does two jobs: it opsonizes the microbe (coats it for phagocytosis) and it builds the next enzyme, C5 convertase.

C5 convertase cleaves C5 into C5a and C5b. C5b recruits C6, C7, C8, and multiple C9s, which polymerize into the membrane attack complex (MAC) — a donut-shaped pore that punches through the microbe's membrane and causes osmotic lysis.

The whole cascade is ruthlessly controlled. C1 inhibitor (C1-INH) blocks the classical pathway. DAF (CD55) and CD59 sit on your own cells' surfaces and prevent MAC assembly on self. Each regulator has a disease attached to its loss — and those diseases are the fastest way to remember which regulator does what.

diagrams.caption: Three pathways converge at C3 convertase. C3b opsonizes and drives C5 convertase. The MAC assembles and drills the membrane. Regulators prevent self-damage — and the diseases are the mirror image of which regulator is missing.

flow.text: Complement = ~30 plasma proteins, circulating inactive.
flow.subtext: Not cells. Not antibodies. Proteins in the blood that activate only when triggered.
flow.insight: Complement is plasma proteins — no cells required

flow.text: Three trigger pathways, one convergence point.
flow.subtext: Classical (antibody-antigen) · Lectin (MBL → mannose) · Alternative (spontaneous, amplified on microbial surfaces).
flow.insight: Three pathways → all converge at C3 convertase

flow.text: C3 is split. C3b opsonizes. C3a inflames.
flow.subtext: C3b coats the microbe for phagocytosis and builds the next enzyme (C5 convertase). C3a is an anaphylatoxin.
flow.insight: C3b = opsonin + amplifier · C3a = anaphylatoxin

flow.text: C5b joins C6–C9 = MAC. The pore lyses the microbe.
flow.subtext: Membrane Attack Complex — a ring of proteins drilled through the membrane. Water rushes in. The bacterium explodes.
flow.insight: MAC = C5b + C6 + C7 + C8 + C9 ring → pore → osmotic lysis

flow.text: Regulators keep complement from attacking self.
flow.subtext: C1-INH brakes classical · DAF + CD59 block MAC on your own red cells · Factor H restrains alternative pathway.
flow.insight: Regulators: C1-INH (classical brake), DAF/CD59 (self MAC shield)

flow.text: Every regulator has a disease. Every convertase has one too.
flow.subtext: Deficiencies map directly to presentations — memorize the map and the board questions solve themselves.
flow.insight: C1-INH↓ = hereditary angioedema · C5-C9↓ = Neisseria · DAF/CD59↓ = PNH · C3↓ = pyogenic + IC disease

stations.bridge: A college student has had three separate episodes of bacterial meningitis in two years — all caused by Neisseria meningitidis. Her immune workup shows normal IgG, IgA, and IgM. Her T- and B-cell counts are normal. But her CH50 (total complement) is low. The pathogen and the pattern together point to a specific piece of complement missing.
stations.vignette:
A 20-year-old college student is hospitalized with her third episode of bacterial meningitis in two years. Cerebrospinal fluid cultures from all three episodes have grown Neisseria meningitidis. Between episodes she is healthy and has no other infections of note. Her CBC is normal. Immunoglobulin levels are normal. T-cell and B-cell subset counts are normal. A CH50 assay, which measures total complement activity, is markedly low.

She is otherwise well-appearing with no family history of autoimmune disease.
stations.stem: A deficiency in which of the following best explains this patient's recurrent Neisseria infections?
stations.explanation:
Neisseria species (meningitidis and gonorrhoeae) are encapsulated gram-negative organisms that are uniquely dependent on the membrane attack complex for clearance. A deficiency in any terminal complement component (C5, C6, C7, C8, or C9) blocks MAC assembly and results in recurrent neisserial infections — the classic board pattern.

C1-INH deficiency (A) causes hereditary angioedema (episodic non-pruritic swelling, often of face and airway), not recurrent infection. C3 deficiency (B) causes recurrent pyogenic infections (e.g., Strep pneumoniae) and immune-complex disease — the infectious picture is broader than neisserial. DAF deficiency (D) causes paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria — red cell lysis, not bacterial infections. MBL deficiency (E) causes more diffuse bacterial infections in infancy, not the narrow Neisseria pattern.

Narrow pattern + Neisseria + low CH50 = MAC deficiency. Every time.
stations.whatsNext:
Complement kills the enemies you can reach — bacteria swimming in plasma, encapsulated organisms coated with IgM. But some enemies can't be reached by circulating proteins: a virus replicating inside one of your own cells, or a tumor cell that looks almost identical to healthy self-tissue.

Those require a very different kind of assassin. One that checks every cell it meets and kills the ones that look suspicious — without needing an antibody or even a prior exposure.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: NK deficiency → severe herpesvirus infection
stations.subtitle: The missing-self killer.
stations.stage: Cell-mediated innate defense against virus-infected and tumor cells
stations.previously: Complement handled what's outside — plasma-protein opsonization and MAC-mediated lysis of free microbes. But what about threats hiding inside host cells? Viruses replicating in your own epithelium. Tumor cells that look like self but aren't behaving like self. For those, the body has a dedicated cellular assassin: the natural killer cell.
stations.cardTitle: A cell that kills without being told.
stations.tagline: No antibody, no prior exposure, no T-cell help. Just look at every cell in the body and ask one question: does this look normal?
stations.story:
Most immune killing is specific. A cytotoxic T cell waits for dendritic cells to present a specific antigen, proliferates for days, then finds and kills infected cells displaying that exact antigen. It's precise but slow — and it only works for antigens the body has already seen.

Natural killer cells (NK cells) work differently. They're large granular lymphocytes that circulate constantly, checking every cell they meet. They kill virus-infected cells and tumor cells without needing prior exposure, without antibody guidance, without help from T cells. They're the innate arm of cell-mediated immunity.

The question is: how does an NK cell, meeting a random cell in tissue, know whether to kill it or leave it alone?

The answer is the missing-self hypothesis. Every healthy human cell constantly displays MHC class I on its surface — a marker that says "I am your own tissue." Viruses and tumors often downregulate MHC-I to hide from CD8 T cells (which require MHC-I to recognize their target). That's clever, but it has a cost: the cell now looks like not-self. And NK cells recognize that.

NK cells carry two kinds of surface receptors:

  • Inhibitory receptors (like KIRs) bind MHC-I. When they engage, they send a "don't kill" signal.
  • Activating receptors (like NKG2D) bind stress-induced ligands that appear on infected or stressed cells. When they engage, they send a "kill" signal.

The NK cell integrates both signals. Inhibitory wins → spare. Activating wins → kill. A healthy cell displays lots of MHC-I and few stress ligands — the inhibitory signal dominates and the cell is spared. An infected or tumor cell has lost MHC-I (inhibitory signal gone) and gained stress ligands (activating signal on) — the cell is killed.

The killing mechanism is the same as a cytotoxic T cell's. The NK cell releases perforin, which polymerizes into a pore in the target cell's membrane, and granzymes, which enter through the pore and trigger apoptosis. The target cell dies a clean, controlled death — without spilling virus or tumor contents.

NK cells have one more trick: ADCC (antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity). NK cells carry the Fc receptor CD16, which binds the tail of IgG antibodies. If a target cell is coated with IgG, the NK cell's CD16 latches on and delivers the same perforin/granzyme kill. This is the bridge between innate and adaptive — NK cells use antibodies that B cells made, but they do it innately, without the days-long T-cell activation pathway.

diagrams.caption: Healthy cell: lots of MHC-I, inhibitory signal dominates, NK spares it. Infected/tumor cell: MHC-I lost, activating signal wins, NK kills it via perforin + granzyme.

flow.text: The problem: some threats hide inside host cells.
flow.subtext: Viruses replicate inside your own epithelium. Tumor cells look nearly identical to healthy self. Complement and antibodies can't reach them.
flow.insight: Viruses + tumor cells hide inside host cells — unreachable by complement / antibodies

flow.text: The NK cell: large granular lymphocyte. No antibody, no prior exposure needed.
flow.subtext: Part of the innate immune system. Constantly circulates. Checks every cell. Born knowing how to kill.
flow.insight: NK cell = innate cellular killer · no prior exposure required

flow.text: Missing-self hypothesis: healthy cells show MHC-I. Infected / tumor cells often don't.
flow.subtext: Viruses and tumors downregulate MHC-I to hide from CD8 T cells — but then NK cells notice the absence.
flow.insight: MHC-I present = healthy / MHC-I absent = suspicious

flow.text: Two receptors, one decision: inhibitory vs activating.
flow.subtext: KIRs (inhibitory) bind MHC-I → don't kill. NKG2D (activating) binds stress ligands → kill. Strongest signal wins.
flow.insight: Inhibitory (MHC-I) vs Activating (stress ligands) — strongest signal wins

flow.text: Kill mechanism: perforin pore + granzyme → apoptosis.
flow.subtext: Perforin polymerizes a pore. Granzymes enter and activate caspases. Clean, controlled death — no inflammatory spill.
flow.insight: Perforin pore → granzyme entry → caspase activation → apoptosis

flow.text: ADCC: the Fc receptor bridges innate and adaptive.
flow.subtext: CD16 on NK cells binds IgG Fc regions. If a cell is antibody-coated, NK grabs it and kills via the same perforin-granzyme pathway.
flow.insight: ADCC: CD16 binds IgG-coated targets → same perforin/granzyme kill

stations.bridge: A 14-year-old girl has had severe, recurrent herpesvirus infections since infancy — disseminated HSV as an infant, severe VZV as a toddler, CMV mononucleosis in childhood. Her antibody levels are normal. Her T-cell counts are normal. But on flow cytometry, one lymphocyte population is nearly absent. The pattern — bad at herpes, good at everything else — points to the deficit.
stations.vignette:
A 14-year-old girl is referred to immunology after a third severe herpesvirus infection — this time CMV mononucleosis requiring hospitalization. Her history includes disseminated neonatal HSV at 4 weeks of age and severe varicella with pneumonitis at age 3. Between herpesvirus episodes she is well, with no recurrent bacterial infections, no opportunistic fungi, and no autoimmune disease.

Lab studies show normal total IgG, IgA, and IgM. T-cell subset counts (CD3+, CD4+, CD8+) are all within normal range. Flow cytometry for the CD16+/CD56+ lymphocyte population shows this population is nearly absent.
stations.stem: A deficiency in which of the following cell types best explains this patient's pattern of infections?
stations.explanation:
The distinctive pattern here is severe and recurrent herpesvirus infections (HSV, VZV, CMV) in a patient with otherwise normal immune parameters. NK cells are the primary innate defense against herpesviruses — they kill virus-infected cells via the missing-self mechanism and via ADCC. Patients with isolated NK cell deficiency classically present with disseminated herpesvirus infections from infancy.

The flow cytometry finding — absent CD16+/CD56+ lymphocytes — directly identifies NK cells.

B cell deficiency (A) causes recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections and low immunoglobulins — both absent here. CD4 T-cell deficiency (B) produces opportunistic infections (PCP, disseminated fungi, atypical mycobacteria), not isolated herpesvirus disease. Neutrophil defects (C) cause pyogenic bacterial and fungal infections — not viral. Plasmacytoid dendritic cell deficiency (E) impairs type I interferon production and could overlap clinically, but they are not CD16+/CD56+ and would not be the population identified on that flow panel.

Severe herpes + normal everything else + absent CD16/56 = NK deficiency.
stations.whatsNext: You've now seen both arms of systemic innate defense — complement (plasma-protein, kills extracellular bugs) and NK cells (cellular, kills infected / tumor cells). The filters in the blood are next: the spleen you already met, and the detective clue it leaves behind when it's missing.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Howell-Jolly bodies → functional asplenia
stations.subtitle: Howell-Jolly bodies.
stations.previously: The spleen filters blood, catches encapsulated organisms, and runs an antibody factory against them. Its absence is catastrophic — but how do you know an absence is there? One way is elegant: look at a blood smear.
stations.tagline: They should have been pitted out. They weren't. Why?
stations.story:
RBCs are made in the bone marrow with a nucleus, which is extruded before the cell enters circulation. Occasionally — when production is rushed (hemolytic states, sickle cell) or the extrusion is imperfect — tiny fragments of nuclear material remain behind. Small, round, dense basophilic inclusions inside an otherwise normal-looking red cell. These are Howell-Jolly bodies.

In a healthy person, Howell-Jolly bodies don't accumulate — because when an RBC with a nuclear remnant passes through the spleen, splenic macrophages recognize the inclusion and "pit" it out. The RBC continues on, now normal.

So if Howell-Jolly bodies are present on a peripheral smear, one of two things is true:

  1. The spleen has been surgically removed
  2. The spleen is not functioning — functional asplenia (classic in sickle cell disease after repeated auto-infarctions)

Either way, the immunologic risk profile is identical: no filter, no IgM memory B cell help, maximum susceptibility to encapsulated organisms.

When a board question mentions Howell-Jolly bodies, the answer is almost always about asplenia, and the organism to worry about is almost always S. pneumoniae.

diagrams.caption: Where Howell-Jolly bodies come from (incomplete nuclear extrusion) and where the spleen normally sends them (plucked out by sinusoidal macrophages).

explanationDiagrams.caption: Top: the smear view. Middle: the pitting mechanism that normally removes Howell-Jolly bodies. Below: the two causes of asplenia. The clinical answer chain always lands on S. pneumoniae.

flow.text: RBCs start life nucleated. They extrude the nucleus as they mature.
flow.subtext: Bone marrow erythroid precursors have nuclei. Before leaving marrow, they spit out the nucleus.
flow.insight: Mature RBC = no nucleus, by design

flow.text: Sometimes a small nuclear fragment is left behind.
flow.subtext: Incomplete extrusion. Tiny piece of DNA remains inside. This is a Howell-Jolly body.
flow.insight: Howell-Jolly = leftover DNA fragment in RBC

flow.text: A healthy spleen plucks these fragments out.
flow.subtext: Splenic red pulp macrophages line the sinusoids. As RBCs squeeze through, H-J bodies get pitted out.
flow.insight: Splenic pitting = removal of H-J bodies from RBCs

flow.prompt: You see Howell-Jolly bodies on the smear. What does that TELL you about the patient?

flow.text: H-J bodies on smear → spleen is not filtering.
flow.subtext: Either absent (surgical splenectomy) or not working (functional asplenia). Both equally dangerous.
flow.insight: H-J bodies visible = spleen not doing its job

flow.text: Classic cause: sickle cell disease → autosplenectomy.
flow.subtext: Repeat sickling infarcts the spleen over years. By adolescence, the spleen is a fibrotic scar. Functionally absent.
flow.insight: Sickle cell → autosplenectomy → H-J + encapsulated risk

flow.question: Howell-Jolly bodies on peripheral smear mean the patient is most at risk for —

choices.text: Overwhelming infection with encapsulated bacteria (e.g. S. pneumoniae)

feedback.correct: Right. H-J bodies = functional asplenia = loss of both the filter and IgM memory B cells. Encapsulated organisms — especially S. pneumoniae — are the biggest danger.
feedback.wrong: EBV and intracellular bacteria are controlled by T cells — not spleen-dependent. The asplenic vulnerability is specifically to ENCAPSULATED organisms handled by Ab + complement.

flow.text: Other causes: surgical splenectomy, splenic infarct, cirrhosis with congestion.
flow.subtext: Any process that stops macrophages from pitting DNA fragments will cause H-J bodies to appear.
flow.insight: Causes: sickle cell, splenectomy, infarct, cirrhosis

flow.text: Next station: the tempo of infection in the asplenic patient.
flow.subtext: No filter. No specific antibody. How fast can things fall apart? Hours.
flow.insight: Next: OPSI — 12 hours from 'feeling off' to septic shock

stations.bridge: A child with sickle cell disease. His smear shows Howell-Jolly bodies. Which infection is he most at risk for?
stations.vignette: A 7-year-old boy with homozygous sickle cell disease is evaluated during a well-child visit. He has been maintained on hydroxyurea and penicillin prophylaxis since infancy. Peripheral blood smear reveals sickled erythrocytes along with round, basophilic inclusions within otherwise normal-appearing red blood cells.
stations.stem: The finding described on peripheral blood smear places this patient at greatest risk for infection with which of the following organisms?
stations.explanation:
Howell-Jolly bodies indicate functional asplenia — here from chronic autoinfarction in sickle cell disease. The patient is most at risk for encapsulated organisms, with S. pneumoniae being the leading cause of life-threatening infection in this population.

CMV (A) and Pneumocystis (C) are T-cell-deficiency concerns. TB (B) requires cell-mediated immunity. Pseudomonas (D) is a neutropenia concern.
stations.whatsNext: Now let's see what happens when an asplenic patient actually gets infected — and why it's so terrifyingly fast.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: Post-splenectomy sepsis (OPSI)
stations.subtitle: OPSI.
stations.previously: You now know what the spleen does and how to detect its absence on a blood smear. Now here's what asplenic sepsis actually looks like — and it's fast.
stations.tagline: A patient can go from mild malaise to DIC in hours.
stations.story:
A 29-year-old woman comes to the ED with sudden fever, chills, and confusion. Six years ago, splenectomy after trauma. She's been healthy since. Today — BP 82/50, thrombocytopenic, PT and aPTT prolonged, D-dimer high. Classic disseminated intravascular coagulation.

This is overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI), and the time course is what makes it so dangerous. A patient can go from "feeling a bit off" to septic shock and multi-organ failure in under 12 hours. Lifetime risk after splenectomy is ~5%, but when it happens mortality is 50% or more even with modern ICU care.

The organism, more often than not, is S. pneumoniae. Can be H. influenzae or N. meningitidis, but pneumococcus dominates in adults. It commonly starts as a seemingly minor upper respiratory illness — a "cold" — and escalates because the usual filtering and antibody clearance steps have been disabled.

This is why asplenic patients are counseled:

  • Treat any fever as a medical emergency
  • Take amoxicillin-clavulanate at the first sign of fever
  • Get to an ED without delay
  • Receive pneumococcal, meningococcal, and Hib vaccines (all non-negotiable)
  • Many take daily prophylactic penicillin, especially children

Antibiotics started within the first few hours change outcomes. Antibiotics started late often don't.

diagrams.caption: The clinical tempo (hours, not days), the trigger cascade (minor URI to unchecked bacteremia), and the pathophysiology of DIC that explains every lab finding.

explanationDiagrams.caption: The crash is fast because two defenses are missing at once: IgM memory B cell antibody and splenic macrophage filtration. Bacteria multiply in blood before the host can respond.

flow.text: A minor trigger: URI, cold, sinus infection.
flow.subtext: Something seeds nasopharyngeal bacteria into blood. Normally no big deal. In asplenia, catastrophic.
flow.insight: OPSI trigger: minor colonization event / URI

flow.text: Normally, the spleen catches bacteria in seconds.
flow.subtext: Every heartbeat sends blood through splenic sinusoids. Encapsulated bugs get opsonized and removed.
flow.insight: Normal spleen: bacteremia → cleared in minutes

flow.text: No spleen → bacteria double every ~30 minutes.
flow.subtext: No filter removing them. No targeted IgM. Bacteremia is exponential.
flow.insight: Without spleen: bacteria grow exponentially in blood

flow.prompt: What's the final common pathway that makes OPSI lethal?

flow.text: Massive bacteremia → cytokine storm → sepsis.
flow.subtext: TNF, IL-1, IL-6 flood the body. Vasodilation. Shock. Endothelium activated everywhere.
flow.insight: Cytokine storm → systemic sepsis

flow.text: Endothelium activated everywhere → DIC.
flow.subtext: Disseminated clotting in small vessels. Consumes platelets + clotting factors. Simultaneous thrombosis AND bleeding.
flow.insight: DIC: clots + bleeds in parallel

flow.question: The asplenic patient with sepsis has a rapidly falling platelet count and new oozing from IV sites. Why both at once?

choices.text: DIC: consumption of platelets and clotting factors by widespread microthrombi

feedback.correct: Exactly. Endothelial damage triggers the coagulation cascade system-wide. Platelets and fibrinogen get consumed in microthrombi, then the patient bleeds from whatever site is already open.
feedback.wrong: HIT and autoimmune thrombocytopenia don't cause active bleeding alongside thrombosis like DIC does. Sepsis + asplenia = consumptive coagulopathy is the classic mechanism.

flow.text: 12 hours from feeling-off to septic shock.
flow.subtext: Antibiotics given within the first few hours change outcomes. Given late, often don't.
flow.insight: Clinical window: ~hours, not days. Treat early.

flow.text: Next: the lifelong plan for an asplenic patient.
flow.subtext: How do we prevent OPSI in the first place? Vaccines. Prophylaxis. Education.
flow.insight: Next: multi-decade asplenic care plan

stations.bridge: An asplenic young woman crashing with sepsis and DIC. What explains the speed?
stations.vignette: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with fever, chills, and confusion that began 6 hours ago. She had a splenectomy 6 years earlier after a motor vehicle collision. BP is 80/48 mm Hg. Labs show platelets 42,000/μL, prolonged PT and aPTT, and elevated D-dimer. Blood cultures are drawn.
stations.stem: Which of the following best explains this patient's rapid clinical deterioration?

choices.text: Absence of IgM memory B cells and splenic clearance of encapsulated bacteria
Impaired T-cell activation against intracellular pathogens
Reduced complement activity from autoimmune destruction

stations.explanationTitle: Answer · A · Loss of splenic filtration and IgM memory B cells
stations.explanation:
OPSI is driven by loss of two synergistic functions of the spleen: (1) mechanical filtering of opsonized bacteria from blood, and (2) the IgM memory B cell response that produces early protective antibody against encapsulated organisms. Without both, pneumococcus entering the bloodstream multiplies unchecked — hence the 12-hour sepsis timeline.

Thrombocytopenia (B) is the opposite — splenectomy usually causes thrombocytosis. Hypogammaglobulinemia (C), T-cell defects (D), and complement issues (E) aren't caused by splenectomy.
stations.whatsNext: Does the same risk apply to a child — someone whose adaptive immunity is still developing?

bank.label: Bonus · The prophylactic antibiotic
bank.vignette: A patient is counseled that after splenectomy she should keep amoxicillin-clavulanate available and take it at the first sign of fever, even before evaluation.
bank.stem: The rationale for this self-administered antibiotic is best explained by which principle?

choices.text: Encapsulated bacteria resist phagocytosis unless pre-opsonized
Gram-negative rods require early carbapenem coverage
Without a spleen, complement is inactivated

bank.explanation: The rationale is that splenic function is needed to clear encapsulated organisms, and once bacteremia begins, time-to-antibiotic is the most important predictor of survival.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Vaccination + prophylaxis in asplenic children
stations.subtitle: Lifelong risk.
stations.previously: An adult without a spleen faces catastrophic sepsis risk. Does the same apply to a child? The mechanism doesn't care about age — but the pediatric case has one extra wrinkle.
stations.cardTitle: What her parents should watch for.
stations.tagline: It isn't CMV. It isn't lymphoma. It's one specific class of bacteria.
stations.story:
A 5-year-old girl had her spleen removed six months ago after traumatic abdominal injury. Her parents ask the right question at follow-up: "What should we watch for?"

The answer is the same as for the 29-year-old adult in the previous station. Mechanism doesn't care about age.

The long-term risk — for the rest of her life — is severe bacterial infection with encapsulated organisms. She needs:

  • The full vaccination series, including boosters
  • Education that fevers are emergencies
  • A medical alert bracelet (so an ED team knows her status if she's unconscious)
  • Prophylactic antibiotics, at least for several years

There's one reason to separately consider the pediatric case: children under 5 are at particularly high risk of OPSI, because their adaptive immune systems haven't fully built antibody responses to encapsulated organisms yet. Wider risk window. Greater need for prophylaxis.

Students occasionally guess at wrong answers — CMV, lymphoma, lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia. None of those are specific concerns of asplenia. CMV is a T-cell deficiency issue. Lymphoma isn't caused by splenectomy. And splenectomy typically causes thrombocytosis (high platelets), not thrombocytopenia, because the spleen normally sequesters about a third of circulating platelets.

The one thing to worry about, above all others, is bacterial infection — and S. pneumoniae is the organism that costs the most sleep.

diagrams.caption: Age-based immune maturation, the 4-step lifelong plan set against a decades timeline, and the physiology of platelet sequestration.

flow.text: Children under ~5 have immature adaptive immunity.
flow.subtext: They rely more heavily on spleen for encapsulated defense. Remove the spleen in a young child → highest-risk window.
flow.insight: Young kids = most dependent on splenic function

flow.text: Plan #1: vaccinate against the 3 big encapsulated bugs.
flow.subtext: Pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23). H. influenzae type b. Meningococcal (MenACWY + MenB).
flow.insight: Asplenic vaccine trio: pneumo + Hib + meningo

flow.text: Plan #2: daily prophylactic penicillin (at least through age 5).
flow.subtext: Oral penicillin VK daily. Some centers continue longer in high-risk patients.
flow.insight: Daily PO penicillin through early childhood

flow.prompt: The child still goes out in the world — to school, on trips. How do the parents manage a sudden fever?

flow.text: Plan #3: emergency antibiotics on hand.
flow.subtext: Parents carry a dose of oral amoxicillin-clavulanate (or similar). ANY fever → take dose AND go straight to ER.
flow.insight: Stand-by antibiotics at home + immediate ER

flow.text: Plan #4: educate parents that fever = EMERGENCY.
flow.subtext: Not a wait-and-see. Not a let’s-see-if-it-resolves. The window between URI-like symptoms and septic shock is hours.
flow.insight: Asplenic fever = no wait-and-see

flow.question: Side effect of splenectomy to explain to parents: which way does the platelet count go?

choices.text: Up (thrombocytosis — spleen normally sequesters ~1/3 of platelets)

feedback.correct: Right. The spleen normally holds about a third of circulating platelets. Remove it, and they all go to the bloodstream — expect thrombocytosis.
feedback.wrong: Trick question. Splenectomy causes thrombocytOSIS (high) because the spleen normally sequesters platelets. A common distractor on exams.

flow.text: Side effect: thrombocytosis, not thrombocytopenia.
flow.subtext: Spleen sequesters ~1/3 of platelets. Remove it → platelets all released to circulation. Levels rise, sometimes markedly.
flow.insight: Splenectomy → HIGH platelets (not low)

flow.text: Next: neutropenia — a totally different kind of immunodeficiency.
flow.subtext: Spleen problems = antibody/complement vulnerability. Neutrophil problems = different bugs, different fears.
flow.insight: Next: what goes wrong when NEUTROPHILS disappear

stations.bridge: A pediatric follow-up visit. The right long-term complication to emphasize to her parents is…
stations.vignette: A 5-year-old girl underwent splenectomy 6 months ago following a traumatic abdominal injury from a bicycle accident. She has recovered well and has no complaints today. Her parents ask what they should monitor for in the long term.
stations.stem: Which of the following is the most important long-term complication to discuss with her parents?
stations.explanation:
Lifelong asplenia → lifelong risk of OPSI with encapsulated organisms, especially S. pneumoniae. Children under 5 are at the highest risk due to immature humoral immunity, making vaccination and prophylactic antibiotics essential.

Lymphopenia (A), CMV (B), lymphoma (C), and thrombocytopenia (E) are not specific concerns of asplenia. Splenectomy typically causes thrombocytosis, not thrombocytopenia.
stations.whatsNext: Asplenia is one kind of systemic defense loss. There's another — lose enough neutrophils in circulation, and you face a different vulnerability entirely.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Pseudomonas as first-line empiric coverage
stations.subtitle: Febrile neutropenia.
stations.previously: Asplenic patients are vulnerable to encapsulated organisms. Neutropenic patients are vulnerable to something different — gram-negative rods, especially Pseudomonas.
stations.cardTitle: No neutrophils, no pus, no classic signs.
stations.tagline: A raging pneumonia can look like a mostly clean chest X-ray.
stations.story:
A 59-year-old man with acute leukemia starts combination chemotherapy. Eight days later: fever, rigors, no obvious source on exam. White count is 200/μL — essentially zero. ANC is negligible. Blood cultures grow Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

This is febrile neutropenia with gram-negative bacteremia, and it's another medical emergency. The mechanism is beautifully direct: chemotherapy wipes out the bone marrow for days to weeks. Without mature segmented neutrophils in circulation, the patient has no effective acute defense against bacteria. Gram-negative rods — especially Pseudomonas, which colonizes hospital surfaces, water, even the patient's own gut — can translocate across injured mucosa and multiply unchecked.

Notice what you don't see in febrile neutropenia. No pus. No redness. No purulent sputum. You need neutrophils to produce those signs. Without them, a raging pneumonia can look like a mostly clean chest X-ray. A raging skin infection looks like mild erythema. The body can't mount a pus-forming response when the cells that make pus aren't there.

This is why oncologists have a low threshold for empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics in any neutropenic fever: Pseudomonas coverage specifically (cefepime, pip-tazo, meropenem) starts before cultures return. Waiting for diagnosis is too slow.

Landscape map worth memorizing:

  • Complement deficiencies → Neisseria infections
  • Immunoglobulin deficiencies → recurrent sinopulmonary (encapsulated organisms)
  • T-cell deficiencies → viral, fungal, opportunistic (like PCP)
  • Neutrophil deficiencies → acute bacterial, especially gram-negative rods, especially Pseudomonas

Different defenses, different vulnerabilities.

diagrams.caption: Drug → marrow → blood → tissue. Each scene shows the causal step. Top row: the healthy neutrophil supply chain. Bottom row: what chemotherapy breaks.

flow.text: Chemotherapy kills rapidly dividing cells.
flow.subtext: Tumor cells divide fast. So do marrow precursors, gut epithelium, hair follicles. Collateral damage across the board.
flow.insight: Chemo kills all fast-dividing cells (not just tumor)

flow.text: The bone marrow is hit hard.
flow.subtext: All 3 lineages suppressed: RBCs, platelets, neutrophils. But neutrophils have the shortest half-life (~6-8h) — they crash first.
flow.insight: Neutrophils crash fastest (short half-life)

flow.text: ANC drops to near zero by day 7-10.
flow.subtext: Absolute Neutrophil Count < 500/μL = severe neutropenia. < 100/μL = profound. No cells = no pus = atypical presentations.
flow.insight: ANC < 500 = severe; ANC < 100 = profound

flow.prompt: A neutropenic patient spikes a fever. Why is this an emergency, when a fever in a healthy person is usually not?

flow.text: No neutrophils → gut/skin flora invade unopposed.
flow.subtext: Gut bacteria translocate across damaged mucosa. Skin flora enter through IV lines. No PMNs to stop them.
flow.insight: Neutropenic fever → flora translocation / line infection

flow.text: Classic organism: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (gram-negative rod).
flow.subtext: Plus gram-positives from central line (coag-neg staph, S. aureus). Plus gut E. coli/Klebsiella. Plus fungi (Candida) after prolonged courses.
flow.insight: Febrile neutropenia bugs: Pseudomonas, gram+ from lines, gut Gram-

flow.question: A man with ANC of 50 after chemo spikes a fever. A gram-negative rod grows in blood cultures. Most likely organism?

feedback.correct: Exactly. Gram-negative rod in the febrile neutropenic patient = Pseudomonas classically. Empirical antipseudomonal coverage (cefepime, pip-tazo, or carbapenem) is started before cultures return.
feedback.wrong: S. pneumoniae is the asplenia classic (gram-positive diplococci, not GNR). TB doesn't typically present as acute fever in a neutropenic patient. GNR + chemo fever = think Pseudomonas first.

flow.text: Treatment: broad-spectrum antipseudomonal ASAP.
flow.subtext: Cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or carbapenem. Don't wait for cultures. Start within an hour of presentation.
flow.insight: Empirical anti-Pseudomonal antibiotics within 1 hour

flow.text: Next: what if neutropenia is congenital, not iatrogenic?
flow.subtext: An infant with isolated low ANC. Same vulnerability, different workup. Pattern recognition time.
flow.insight: Next: congenital isolated neutropenia in an infant

stations.bridge: A man with zero neutrophils after chemo spikes a fever and grows a gram-negative rod. Which organism fits this pattern?
