stations.vignette: A 59-year-old man with acute myelogenous leukemia is day 8 of induction chemotherapy. He develops fever to 39.1°C and chills but has no localizing symptoms and a normal chest examination. His total WBC is 200/μL with an absolute neutrophil count of 50/μL. Blood cultures drawn on admission grow a gram-negative rod.
stations.stem: The most likely causative organism is which of the following?
stations.explanation:
Profound neutropenia creates specific vulnerability to gram-negative rods, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the classic and most feared pathogen in febrile neutropenia — hence anti-pseudomonal empiric therapy (cefepime, pip-tazo, meropenem) before cultures return.

E. coli (A) is possible but less specific to neutropenia. H. influenzae (B) and N. meningitidis (C) are encapsulated, more relevant in asplenia/humoral defects. S. pneumoniae (E) is gram-positive.
stations.whatsNext: Neutropenia from chemotherapy is acquired. Some patients are neutropenic from birth — and the clinical picture is surprisingly quiet.

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Cell-line half-life → which drops first
stations.subtitle: Congenital neutropenia.
stations.previously: Acquired neutropenia from chemotherapy causes dramatic infections. What about a baby with persistently low neutrophils from birth?
stations.tagline: The pattern of the deficit narrows the differential.
stations.story:
A 6-week-old infant is brought in after routine labs show persistently low white counts. Born at term, no complications. Feeding well, gaining weight, no history of recurrent infection. Exam unremarkable.

Serial bloodwork: normal hemoglobin, normal platelets, normal lymphocytes, persistently decreased absolute neutrophil count since birth.

What is this?

It's congenital neutropenia — an inherited disorder of neutrophil production. The key phrase in the clinical picture is "isolated neutropenia": only neutrophils are affected, not RBCs or platelets or lymphocytes. And "since birth, consistently" distinguishes this from acquired causes.

Why recognize this? Because it's easy to jump to wrong answers:

  • Alloimmune hemolytic disease of the newborn causes anemia, not neutropenia
  • Congenital CMV usually has microcephaly, hearing loss, petechiae, jaundice
  • DiGeorge causes T-cell deficiency (thymic aplasia), plus cardiac defects and hypocalcemia
  • SCID causes combined T + B cell deficiency with severe early infections

None of those fit. An infant with isolated persistent neutropenia since birth and normal everything else has congenital neutropenia until proven otherwise.

The general principle is worth remembering: which cell line is low tells you which compartment has a problem. Low neutrophils alone → neutrophil problem. Low everything → stem cell / bone marrow problem. Low lymphocytes specifically → lymphoid lineage problem. Use the pattern of the deficit to narrow your differential.

diagrams.caption: The hematopoietic lineage tree and three patterns of failure at different anatomic levels. The pattern of the CBC tells you which compartment is broken.

flow.text: When a patient has infections, first ask: what's low on the CBC?
flow.subtext: The pattern of the cytopenia narrows the differential before you even know the mutation.
flow.insight: Pattern-matching: which cell line is missing?

flow.text: Everything low → stem cell / marrow failure.
flow.subtext: Pancytopenia: RBCs, platelets, neutrophils all down. Think aplastic anemia, myelodysplasia, or marrow replacement.
flow.insight: Pancytopenia → stem cell / marrow problem

flow.text: Only lymphocytes low → lymphoid lineage problem.
flow.subtext: Selective lymphopenia. Think SCID in infants, HIV, congenital combined immunodeficiencies.
flow.insight: Isolated lymphopenia → lymphoid-specific defect

flow.prompt: If just the neutrophils are low (and everything else is normal), where is the defect?

flow.text: Only neutrophils low → neutrophil lineage-specific defect.
flow.subtext: Myeloid progenitor problem affecting only the granulocyte line. Or circulation / destruction issue at the mature level.
flow.insight: Isolated neutropenia → myeloid lineage defect

flow.text: Classic cause in an infant: Kostmann syndrome (severe congenital neutropenia).
flow.subtext: Autosomal recessive. ELANE or HAX1 mutations. ANC typically < 200/μL from birth. Recurrent severe bacterial infections. Cyclic neutropenia is another option (21-day cycles).
flow.insight: Kostmann (ELANE/HAX1) = congenital severe neutropenia

flow.question: A 4-month-old with recurrent skin abscesses, oral ulcers, and ANC of 150. Hgb and platelets normal. Best dx?

feedback.correct: Yes. Isolated neutropenia (everything else normal) + infant + recurrent bacterial = Kostmann / severe congenital neutropenia. Treat with G-CSF.
feedback.wrong: SCID affects LYMPHOCYTES specifically (would show T/B cell deficits). Aplastic anemia causes PANCYTOPENIA (all lines low). Isolated neutropenia in an infant points squarely at Kostmann.

flow.text: Clinical pattern: recurrent bacterial + fungal from birth.
flow.subtext: Skin abscesses, oral ulcers, perirectal infections, pneumonia. Staph aureus and gram-negative rods common. Fungi later.
flow.insight: Kostmann: bacterial + fungal infections from infancy

flow.text: You've closed the systemic story. Next chapter: the long siege.
flow.subtext: Chapter 4 shifts from neutrophils to macrophages and T cells. Tuberculosis. Granulomas. Chronic infections that last years, not hours.
flow.insight: Next chapter: macrophage + T cell battles (TB, granulomas)

stations.bridge: An infant with isolated low ANC and no other abnormalities — which diagnosis fits best?
stations.vignette: A 6-week-old girl is evaluated after routine bloodwork shows a low white blood cell count. She was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. She is feeding and growing well and has had no fevers or infections. Physical exam is normal. Complete blood count shows hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, platelets 312,000/μL, lymphocytes within normal range, and absolute neutrophil count of 600/μL (low). Repeat testing over several weeks confirms persistent isolated neutropenia.
stations.stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Alloimmune hemolytic disease of the newborn

stations.explanation:
The pattern fits exactly: isolated neutropenia since birth with normal Hb, platelets, and lymphocytes and no history of recurrent infection. This is classic congenital neutropenia.

Alloimmune hemolytic disease (A) causes anemia. Congenital CMV (B) presents with microcephaly, hearing loss, petechiae, jaundice. DiGeorge (D) causes T-cell deficiency with cardiac defects and hypocalcemia. SCID (E) causes combined T and B cell deficiency with severe infections — not this clinical picture.
stations.whatsNext:
The local fight, the vascular story, the systemic defenses. Every infection so far has been a short fight — bacterium arrives, immune system responds, bacterium dies, story ends in hours or days.

But some pathogens don't die quickly. Some hide inside your own cells, where antibodies can't reach. Some survive inside the very macrophages sent to destroy them. For those, the body needs a different kind of response. Slower. More sustained. Architectural.

It needs granulomas.

chapters.title: Chronic inflammation & granulomas
chapters.subtitle: When the quick response fails, the body settles in — macrophages, T-cell help, granulomas, and the architecture of chronic containment.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: IFN-γ receptor defect → disseminated mycobacteria
stations.subtitle: IFN-γ activation.
stations.previously: Acute inflammation runs in minutes to days. Some microbes — mycobacteria, certain fungi, a handful of intracellular bacteria — don't fit that schedule. They can live inside host cells for years. How does the body handle them?
stations.cardTitle: A resting macrophage can't kill TB.
stations.tagline: It needs a second signal from outside.
stations.story:
A macrophage engulfs a mycobacterium. The phagosome forms. Normally, phagosome fuses with lysosome → phagolysosome → acidic pH and hydrolytic enzymes destroy the contents.

Mycobacteria block this. They prevent phagosome-lysosome fusion. They tolerate acid. Their waxy mycolic-acid cell walls resist the usual killing. A resting macrophage cannot kill M. tuberculosis on its own. It can engulf it. It cannot kill it.

To kill intracellular mycobacteria, the macrophage needs to be activated — transformed into an aggressive state with enhanced lysosomal function, more ROS, better phagosome-lysosome fusion. The signal is interferon-gamma (IFN-γ), from Th1 cells.

The full loop:

  1. Infected macrophage presents mycobacterial antigens on MHC class II to CD4+ T cells
  2. Naive CD4+ cells, plus IL-12 from those macrophages, differentiate into Th1
  3. Th1 cells produce IFN-γ
  4. IFN-γ binds the IFN-γ receptor on macrophages
  5. Macrophages become activated. Now they can actually kill the bacteria inside them.

Innate cells (macrophages) and adaptive cells (T cells) are in constant conversation. Neither handles mycobacteria alone.

Now consider what happens when any part of this loop breaks. A patient with a defective IFN-γ receptor has normal T cells, normal antibody levels, normal B cells — completely normal labs. But their macrophages can't be activated. Exposed to environmental mycobacteria like M. avium complex — which healthy people handle without noticing — they develop disseminated infection. This is Mendelian susceptibility to mycobacterial disease.

diagrams.caption: The immunological synapse. Upper: MHC II docks with TCR, IL-12 crosses the cleft. Lower: Th1 sends IFN-γ back, binds IFN-γ receptor on the macrophage. Break any piece → MSMD.

flow.text: The macrophage eats the mycobacterium, but can't kill it.
flow.subtext: Phagocytosis works fine. Killing fails. Mycobacterial cell wall lipids block phagolysosome acidification.
flow.insight: Mycobacteria survive INSIDE macrophages

flow.text: Macrophage calls for help: displays antigen on MHC II, pumps out IL-12.
flow.subtext: Mycobacterial peptide fragments get loaded onto MHC II on macrophage surface. IL-12 is the recruiting cytokine.
flow.insight: Macrophage signals: MHC II (display) + IL-12 (recruit)

flow.text: A Th1 cell reads the message via TCR.
flow.subtext: CD4+ T cell binds the macrophage's MHC II / peptide complex. IL-12 pushes it toward Th1 phenotype.
flow.insight: Th1 = CD4+ T cell polarized by IL-12 on macrophage

flow.prompt: Th1 now has to tell the macrophage to KILL. What signal does it send back?

flow.text: Th1 fires IFN-γ back at the macrophage.
flow.subtext: Interferon-gamma binds IFN-γ receptor on macrophage. Triggers transcription of antimicrobial genes.
flow.insight: IFN-γ = the kill-now signal to macrophage

flow.text: Activated macrophage: phagolysosome maturation + ROS surge = KILL.
flow.subtext: Phagolysosome finally acidifies. NADPH oxidase reassembles. Nitric oxide produced. Mycobacterium dies.
flow.insight: Activation lets phagolysosome fuse + ROS fire

flow.question: A child has disseminated atypical mycobacteria. Immunoglobulins normal. T-cell counts normal. Where's the defect?

feedback.correct: Exactly. Mendelian Susceptibility to Mycobacterial Disease (MSMD). Mutations in IL-12, IL-12R, IFN-γ, or IFN-γR. T cells are there, antibodies are there — but the macrophage-T cell conversation is broken.
feedback.wrong: With normal Ig and normal T-cell counts, the defect isn't in B cells or complement. Disseminated atypical mycobacteria specifically point to the macrophage-activation axis (IL-12/IFN-γ).

flow.text: Break any piece → MSMD (disseminated mycobacterial infection).
flow.subtext: IL-12 mutation. IL-12R mutation. IFN-γ mutation. IFN-γR mutation. Any break stops the loop.
flow.insight: MSMD = break in IL-12 ↔ Th1 ↔ IFN-γ loop

flow.text: Next: what does a chronically activated macrophage build?
flow.subtext: When killing keeps failing, macrophages wall off the infection. A structure emerges — the granuloma.
flow.insight: Next: pyogenic (PMN) vs granulomatous (macrophage) pattern

stations.bridge: A child with recurrent disseminated atypical mycobacterial infections. Immunoglobulins are normal, T-cell counts are normal. Where is the defect?
stations.vignette: A 9-year-old boy with a history of disseminated Mycobacterium avium complex infection is evaluated for recurrent fevers and weight loss. His quantitative immunoglobulins, CD4 and CD8 T-cell counts, and NK cell numbers are all normal. Whole exome sequencing reveals a mutation in a gene encoding a cytokine receptor on macrophages.
stations.stem: Which receptor is most likely defective in this patient?
stations.explanation:
The clinical picture — disseminated atypical mycobacterial infection with normal T-cell and B-cell numbers — is classic for Mendelian susceptibility to mycobacterial disease, most commonly due to IFN-γ receptor defects. Without IFN-γ signaling, macrophages can't be activated to kill intracellular mycobacteria.

CD40L (A) defect causes hyper-IgM syndrome with sinopulmonary and opportunistic infections. IL-2R (C) defects cause SCID. TLR4 (D) affects LPS recognition. TNFR (E) defects cause different phenotypes.
stations.whatsNext: Before we go further into granulomas, let's look at their opposite — the pyogenic abscess — so the contrast is crystal clear.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Neutrophil-dominant acute inflammation
stations.subtitle: Pyogenic pattern.
stations.previously: The Th1 / IFN-γ / macrophage axis is how the body handles pathogens it can't kill quickly. Before we see what chronic containment looks like, we need to see its opposite: the acute pyogenic pattern.
stations.tagline: Two patterns. Two fates. Two kinds of lesion center.
stations.story:
A 65-year-old smoker with fever, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain. CXR shows a cavitary lesion with an air-fluid level — a lung abscess. You biopsy the center. What do you see?

Mostly dying neutrophils and their contents. The core of a pyogenic (pus-forming) abscess is filled with segmented neutrophils releasing their granule contents — including acid hydrolases (lysosomal enzymes that digest proteins, lipids, nucleic acids at acidic pH).

These enzymes do two things at once:

  1. Help destroy bacteria
  2. Liquefy dead tissue — which is why the abscess center becomes soft and fluid-filled

This is acute suppurative inflammation — the pyogenic pattern. Neutrophil-driven. Enzyme-mediated. Pus-producing.

Now compare that to what's coming in the next stations: granulomatous inflammation, where the center is mostly macrophages and giant cells, and the enemy isn't being destroyed at all — it's being walled off.

Same host, same immune system. Two totally different tissue responses depending on what kind of pathogen is involved:

  • Pyogenic bacteria (easy to kill quickly) → acute suppurative → neutrophil-rich pus
  • Intracellular pathogens (can't be killed) → granulomatous → macrophage-rich walls

Recognizing which pattern is in front of you — on a biopsy, in a case description — is a core pathology skill.

diagrams.caption: Two biopsy fields side-by-side. Left: neutrophil-packed pus center with acid hydrolases liquefying tissue. Right: organized ring of macrophages + giant cell + lymphocyte rim walling off intracellular pathogens.

flow.text: Two patterns of chronic lesion: pyogenic vs granulomatous.
flow.subtext: A biopsy tells you which one. The cellular cast is diagnostic.
flow.insight: Biopsy cell type = which immune arm is active

flow.text: Pyogenic: center = sea of neutrophils, acid hydrolases, liquefaction.
flow.subtext: Extracellular bacteria (S. aureus, Strep, gram-neg rods). PMNs pour in, rupture, release enzymes. Tissue dissolves. Pus fills the cavity.
flow.insight: Pyogenic = PMN pus + liquefactive necrosis

flow.text: Granulomatous: organized ring of macrophages, multinucleate giant cells, lymphocyte rim.
flow.subtext: For intracellular bugs (TB, fungi, Brucella) that hide inside macrophages. Walls them off rather than liquefying.
flow.insight: Granuloma = organized cellular wall around intracellular bug

flow.prompt: Why do intracellular pathogens elicit granulomas instead of pus?

flow.text: Intracellular bugs can't be killed directly — they must be walled off.
flow.subtext: PMN enzymes don't reach bugs hiding inside macrophages. Answer: a cellular cage. Th1 recruits more macrophages that fuse into giant cells.
flow.insight: No direct kill → surround + contain = granuloma

flow.text: Caseating granulomas: center turns cheese-like — the TB signature.
flow.subtext: TB granulomas develop central caseous necrosis. Soft, white, cheese-like. Mycobacterial lipids cause this specific pattern.
flow.insight: Caseous = cheese-like necrosis = TB classic

flow.question: You biopsy a cavitary lung lesion. It's filled with neutrophils and liquefying tissue. Most likely organism class?

feedback.correct: Right. PMN-dominated liquefactive necrosis = pyogenic bacteria. S. aureus is the classic cavitary pneumonia causer (also Klebsiella in alcoholics). TB/fungi would show granulomas, not pus.
feedback.wrong: TB and fungi cause granulomatous inflammation (macrophages, giant cells, possibly caseous necrosis) — not neutrophilic pus. Liquefaction + PMN = extracellular pyogenic.

flow.text: Bug class determines the biopsy pattern.
flow.subtext: Extracellular pyogenic → PMNs + pus. Intracellular (TB, fungi, Brucella) → granuloma + possibly caseation.
flow.insight: Pattern on slide = bug class + right Abx

flow.text: Next: granulomas without any bug at all.
flow.subtext: The Th1 loop can fire in the absence of infection. A disease where granulomas appear in the lungs of a young woman — with no organism to wall off.
flow.insight: Next: sarcoidosis — sterile noncaseating granulomas

stations.bridge: A man with a cavitary lung lesion. You biopsy the center. Which cell type and enzyme class dominate?
stations.vignette: A 65-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with three weeks of productive cough, fever, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. Chest CT reveals a thick-walled cavitary lesion with an air-fluid level in the right upper lobe. Aspiration of the cavity yields thick, foul-smelling, yellow-green purulent material that is sent for culture and histologic examination.
stations.stem: Histologic examination of this aspirate is most likely to show abundant neutrophils releasing which of the following?
stations.explanation:
The center of a pyogenic (pus-forming) abscess contains dying neutrophils releasing acid hydrolases — lysosomal enzymes that digest proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids at acidic pH. These enzymes both kill bacteria and liquefy tissue, which is why the abscess center becomes soft and fluid-filled.

Bradykinin (B) drives the vascular pain response, not tissue liquefaction. IgG (C) is B-cell derived. IFN-γ (D) comes from T cells. TGF-β (E) is involved in fibrosis and repair, not acute pus formation.
stations.whatsNext: Now the opposite. What does a lesion look like when the center isn't being destroyed, but walled off?

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Noncaseating granulomas + hilar adenopathy (sarcoidosis)
stations.subtitle: Sarcoidosis.
stations.previously: Pyogenic abscesses are pus-filled and neutrophil-driven. Granulomas are something else entirely — organized, architectural, and built to contain rather than destroy.
stations.tagline: Epithelioid macrophages, giant cells, a rim of Th1 T cells — and sometimes, central caseation.
stations.story:
A granuloma isn't a random collection of cells. It's built with specific blocks:

  • Epithelioid macrophages: activated macrophages with pink cytoplasm, packed tight — resemble epithelial cells, hence the name
  • Multinucleated giant cells: fused macrophages, many nuclei in one cell. Nuclei at periphery = Langhans. Scattered = foreign body.
  • Rim of lymphocytes: mostly Th1 CD4+ T cells, constantly producing IFN-γ to keep the macrophages active
  • Sometimes central necrosis: when the infection is aggressive (TB), the center dies in a soft, cheese-like way — caseation. When it's not aggressive (sarcoid), the granuloma is noncaseating.

Now meet the archetypal noncaseating-granuloma disease: sarcoidosis.

A 30-year-old woman develops reddish-brown papules on her face, a chronic dry cough, and fatigue. Chest imaging: bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Biopsy of a skin lesion or a hilar node: noncaseating granulomas with epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells.

Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory disease of unknown cause, producing noncaseating granulomas in multiple organs — most commonly lungs and lymph nodes, but also skin, eyes, liver, heart. The granulomas look identical wherever they appear.

Why does the immune system make granulomas in sarcoid without a clear antigen? Best current thinking: some antigen the immune system can't clear but keeps responding to, producing a chronic Th1/macrophage response. That's why corticosteroids work — they suppress the chronic T-cell drive.

Take-home: noncaseating granulomas with epithelioid histiocytes and giant cells = the basic granulomatous pattern. Sarcoid is the classic example, but the pattern shows up in many diseases.

diagrams.caption: The full sarcoidosis picture — the chronic Th1/macrophage loop that never resolves, the multi-system organ distribution, the classic vignette, and the iconic bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR.

flow.text: Sarcoidosis = sterile, noncaseating granulomas in multiple organs.
flow.subtext: No bug. No caseation. Just the Th1/macrophage loop firing without off-switch.
flow.insight: Sarcoid = sterile noncaseating granuloma disease

flow.text: Classic patient: young African-American woman, 20-40.
flow.subtext: Most common demographic by far. Presents with dry cough, fatigue, sometimes rash (erythema nodosum) or eye symptoms (uveitis).
flow.insight: Classic sarcoid demographic: Black women 20-40

flow.text: Iconic finding: bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR.
flow.subtext: Both hilar regions enlarged symmetrically. Hallmark of sarcoid. Often the first clue.
flow.insight: Bilateral hilar LAD = sarcoid signature

flow.prompt: What's driving the granulomas if there's no bug? Trace the loop from macrophage to T cell and back.

flow.text: The Th1 loop runs without an antigen to clear.
flow.subtext: IL-12 from macrophages ↔ IFN-γ from Th1 cells. The loop keeps cycling. Macrophages keep forming granulomas. Trigger unknown.
flow.insight: Self-sustaining Th1/macrophage cycle = sarcoid pathophys

flow.text: Labs: high ACE, hypercalcemia, elevated 1,25-vitamin D.
flow.subtext: Activated macrophages express 1-α-hydroxylase unregulated → active vit D → calcium uptake ↑. ACE comes from granulomas themselves.
flow.insight: Sarcoid labs: ACE ↑, Ca²⁺ ↑, 1,25-D ↑

flow.question: A young Black woman with dry cough, bilateral hilar LAD, and noncaseating granulomas on biopsy. Diagnosis?

feedback.correct: Yes. Classic sarcoid triad: young Black woman + bilateral hilar LAD + NONcaseating granulomas. Confirmed by biopsy with no organism. Often treated with steroids if symptomatic.
feedback.wrong: TB causes CASEATING granulomas and is typically unilateral/apical. Bacterial pneumonia causes PMN-filled consolidation, not granulomas. NONcaseating + bilateral hilar = sarcoid.

flow.text: Multi-organ: lungs, skin, eyes, heart, and more.
flow.subtext: Sarcoid granulomas can appear anywhere — skin (lupus pernio), eyes (uveitis), heart (arrhythmia), CNS, joints. Lung is most common (>90%).
flow.insight: Sarcoid is a multi-system disease — lung #1

flow.text: Next: granulomas with star-shaped centers.
flow.subtext: What if the granuloma has a bizarre shape — stellate neutrophil clusters walled by macrophages? Cat-scratch disease next.
flow.insight: Next: stellate microabscess — cat-scratch / Bartonella

stations.bridge: A young woman with bilateral hilar adenopathy. The biopsy shows what's described below. What's the diagnosis?
stations.vignette: A 30-year-old woman presents with several months of dry cough, fatigue, and reddish-brown nodular lesions on her cheeks and nose. Chest imaging shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy without parenchymal consolidation. A biopsy of a hilar node shows well-formed granulomas composed of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells without central caseation or acid-fast bacilli.
stations.stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
stations.explanation:
Sarcoidosis is the classic cause of noncaseating granulomas in bilateral hilar lymph nodes in a young adult, often with skin involvement. Biopsy showing epithelioid histiocytes and giant cells without caseation or organisms is highly characteristic.

Cat scratch (A) produces granulomas with stellate microabscesses (see next station). TB (C) classically causes caseating granulomas, often with acid-fast bacilli. Histoplasmosis (B) can cause caseating granulomas with fungal organisms on special stains. GPA (E) causes vasculitis with necrotizing granulomas.
stations.whatsNext: Not every granuloma looks identical. Subtle architectural differences can point to a specific pathogen — and the classic example is cat scratch disease.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Stellate microabscess → cat-scratch disease
stations.subtitle: Bartonella.
stations.previously: Noncaseating granulomas in sarcoidosis. Caseating granulomas in TB. But sub-patterns exist — and some are so specific they're nearly pathognomonic for a single organism.
stations.tagline: Almost no other disease produces this exact pattern.
stations.story:
Consider a 22-year-old veterinary technician. She has a painful, swollen axillary lymph node. Several days earlier, she was scratched by a stray kitten. Serology confirms infection with Bartonella henselae.

If you biopsy that axillary node, you'll see something beautiful. Granulomas — epithelioid macrophages, the usual architecture — but in the center of each one, instead of simple macrophages or caseation, there's a stellate microabscess: a small, star-shaped collection of neutrophils surrounded by the macrophage rim.

This is cat scratch disease, caused by Bartonella henselae. The histologic signature — granulomas with stellate microabscesses — is almost pathognomonic. Almost no other disease produces this exact combination.

Why does this infection produce a hybrid pattern — granuloma on the outside, neutrophils on the inside? It's a reflection of how the immune system is responding to this specific bacterium. The organism partially resists phagocytosis and causes chronic lymphadenitis, drawing both neutrophils (for the "acute" component) and macrophages (trying to wall things off).

The broader teaching point: the exact flavor of granulomatous inflammation can point at a specific pathogen.

  • Caseating granulomas → TB (classically)
  • Noncaseating granulomas → sarcoidosis (classically)
  • Stellate microabscesses in granulomas → cat scratch disease
  • Granulomas with eosinophils → certain parasitic infections

Pathologists use these sub-patterns as diagnostic fingerprints.

diagrams.caption: Four granuloma fingerprints side-by-side, then the immune story behind the stellate microabscess: cat scratch → axillary LN → star of neutrophils walled off by macrophages = the hybrid pattern of Bartonella henselae.

flow.text: Each granulomatous infection has a signature pattern.
flow.subtext: TB = caseating. Sarcoid = noncaseating. Bartonella = star-shaped central PMN cluster walled by macrophages.
flow.insight: Granuloma architecture is diagnostic

flow.text: The setup: kitten scratch or bite, 1-2 weeks earlier.
flow.subtext: Bartonella henselae is carried by cats (especially kittens). Inoculation at scratch site. The organism travels via lymphatics.
flow.insight: Cat scratch → Bartonella → regional lymph node

flow.text: 1-2 weeks later: tender regional lymph node.
flow.subtext: Axillary node if scratched on the arm. Inguinal if on the leg. Cervical if on the face. The drainage path tells you which node.
flow.insight: Draining regional node → enlarged + tender

flow.prompt: Biopsy of the node shows a star-shaped center. What's in the star?

flow.text: The star = necrotic neutrophil cluster.
flow.subtext: Neutrophils rush to the organism, die, form a radiating star pattern. Classic stellate microabscess.
flow.insight: Stellate microabscess = dying PMN star

flow.text: Ring of macrophages walls off the star.
flow.subtext: Classic granulomatous wall surrounds the necrotic PMN core. Hybrid pattern: pyogenic center + granulomatous rim.
flow.insight: Bartonella granuloma = PMN star + macrophage wall

flow.question: A young woman has tender axillary adenopathy 2 weeks after a kitten scratch. Biopsy: stellate microabscess walled by macrophages. Organism?

feedback.correct: Yes. Cat-scratch disease. Bartonella henselae. Stellate microabscess + cat exposure + regional tender LAD is nearly pathognomonic.
feedback.wrong: TB causes CASEATING granulomas without stellate microabscesses. S. aureus would cause acute pyogenic LAD, not a granulomatous reaction. Cat scratch + stellate pattern = Bartonella.

flow.text: Lymphogranuloma venereum (Chlamydia trachomatis L1-L3) makes the same pattern.
flow.subtext: Also stellate microabscesses, but in inguinal nodes after a sexual exposure. Same pattern, different bug, different drainage.
flow.insight: Stellate microabscess in inguinal node → think LGV

flow.text: Last station: what happens to T-cell immunity as we age?
flow.subtext: We've seen macrophage-T cell partnerships in action. What happens when the T cell workforce itself starts to shrink?
flow.insight: Next: thymic involution — aging and T-cell decline

stations.bridge: A young woman with tender axillary adenopathy days after a kitten scratch. The biopsy description below is nearly pathognomonic.
stations.vignette: A 22-year-old veterinary technician presents with a tender, enlarged right axillary lymph node that has been growing over 10 days. She recalls being scratched on the right forearm by a stray kitten about two weeks ago; the scratch healed with a small papule. Excisional biopsy of the axillary node demonstrates multiple granulomas, each containing a central star-shaped collection of neutrophils surrounded by a rim of epithelioid histiocytes.
stations.stem: The organism most likely responsible for this histologic pattern is which of the following?
stations.explanation:
Granulomas with stellate microabscesses are the nearly pathognomonic histologic pattern of cat scratch disease, caused by Bartonella henselae. The history of a kitten scratch followed by regional lymphadenitis clinches it.

TB (B) causes caseating granulomas. Pasteurella (C) causes rapid cellulitis after animal bites. Sporothrix (D) causes lymphocutaneous nodules. Toxoplasma (E) causes reactive lymphadenopathy with a different pattern (no stellate microabscesses).
stations.whatsNext: Granulomas all share one thing: the pathogen can't be cleared quickly by antibodies or neutrophils — it needs T cells. Let's look at why that matters when T-cell function declines.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Age-related T-cell decline vs intact antibodies
stations.subtitle: Thymic involution.
stations.previously: Every chronic granulomatous infection shares one requirement: cell-mediated immunity. Which means T cells. Which means anything that impairs T-cell function — including normal aging — changes the rules.
stations.tagline: New T cells stop being made. Intracellular pathogens win.
stations.story:
Microbes that cause chronic granulomatous inflammation share a property: they're either intracellular (living inside host cells) or difficult to kill inside phagolysosomes. Antibodies, which float in extracellular space, can't reach them. Complement can't reach them. Neutrophils, which kill and move on, can't handle them.

The only way to clear these pathogens is cell-mediated immunity — T cells that recognize infected host cells, activate macrophages, and coordinate destruction or containment. CD4+ Th1 providing IFN-γ. CD8+ cytotoxic T cells killing infected host cells directly. This is a different arm of immunity from antibodies.

Here's why this matters clinically. Consider a lab study in which young adult and aged mice are both infected with Chlamydophila (an intracellular bacterium). The aged mice clear it poorly — even though their antibody production is normal.

What changed? Not antibodies. Not B cells. T cells.

As we age, the thymus — where new T cells mature — undergoes involution: it shrinks, fills with fat, and produces fewer and fewer naive T cells. Existing T cells persist for decades, but the capacity to generate new T-cell responses declines. Old people have weaker cell-mediated immunity even when their antibody responses to vaccines remain reasonable.

This is why the elderly are more vulnerable to shingles reactivation, TB reactivation, and certain intracellular pathogens.

The clean teaching split: antibodies handle extracellular pathogens. T cells handle intracellular pathogens. Lose T cells, and you lose the fight against the hidden enemies, not the visible ones.

diagrams.caption: Who handles what · how the thymus changes with age (active tissue replaced by fat) · the Chlamydophila experiment · the classic elderly reactivations (shingles, TB).

flow.text: Two arms of adaptive immunity: antibodies (B cells) and T cells.
flow.subtext: Antibodies = extracellular bugs + toxins. T cells = intracellular bugs + viruses + tumor cells. Different jobs.
flow.insight: Ab handles outside; T cells handle inside

flow.text: T cells are educated in the thymus.
flow.subtext: The thymus is the T-cell training school: positive selection (useful TCRs) and negative selection (auto-reactive clones deleted).
flow.insight: Thymus = T cell school (positive + negative selection)

flow.text: With age, the thymus involutes: active tissue → fat.
flow.subtext: Peaks at puberty, shrinks dramatically after. By age 70, most of what was thymus is replaced by adipose.
flow.insight: Aging → thymic tissue replaced by fat

flow.prompt: The thymus is barely functional by age 70. What part of immunity is MOST affected?

flow.text: Result: T-cell immunity declines with age.
flow.subtext: Fewer naive T cells. Narrower TCR repertoire. Reduced Th1 support for macrophages. Antibody levels stay relatively intact.
flow.insight: Aging: T cells decline, antibodies relatively preserved

flow.text: Elderly reactivations: shingles, TB.
flow.subtext: Zoster (VZV) reactivates when T-cell surveillance wanes. Latent TB can reactivate decades later. Intracellular bugs exploit the T-cell gap.
flow.insight: Aging → reactivation of latent intracellular bugs

flow.question: Aged mice fail to clear intracellular Chlamydophila. Antibody levels normal. Which compartment has failed?

feedback.correct: Correct. Intracellular pathogens need T cell / macrophage control. Normal antibodies rules out B cell failure. Age-associated T-cell decline explains the phenotype — same mechanism as shingles/TB reactivation in elderly humans.
feedback.wrong: Normal antibody levels rules out B cell failure. Complement primarily handles extracellular bugs and capsules. Intracellular pathogen + age + normal Ig = T cell / thymic involution.

flow.text: Same principle in humans: elderly need extra protection against intracellular threats.
flow.subtext: Zoster vaccine (Shingrix) recommended ≥ 50. Annual flu shot. Pneumococcal (even though encapsulated, pneumo defense is partly T-dependent).
flow.insight: Age-based vaccines target T cell-controlled pathogens

flow.text: You've laid the foundation of immunology.
flow.subtext: From the first PMN sliding down a vessel, to the macrophage-Th1 conversation, to the granulomas of chronic disease, to how the whole system ages. The innate engine is yours.
flow.insight: Module 1 complete — innate immunity foundation built

stations.bridge: A research finding: aged mice clear intracellular Chlamydophila poorly. Their antibody levels are normal. What's the failing compartment?
stations.vignette: In a laboratory study, young adult and aged mice are both inoculated with Chlamydophila pneumoniae, an obligate intracellular bacterium. Antibody titers against the organism are measured and are equivalent in both groups. However, the aged mice exhibit significantly delayed bacterial clearance from their tissues compared to the young adult mice.
stations.stem: The impaired clearance in aged mice is most likely due to decline in which of the following?
stations.explanation:
Intracellular bacteria like Chlamydophila are cleared primarily by T-cell-mediated immunity — CD4+ Th1 cells activating macrophages via IFN-γ, and CD8+ cytotoxic T cells killing infected cells. With aging, thymic involution reduces the output of naive T cells, weakening this arm of immunity while antibody responses remain relatively preserved.

B-cell class switching (A) and complement (B) support humoral immunity, which (per the question stem) is intact. NK (C) and neutrophils (D) play limited roles against obligate intracellular bacteria.
stations.whatsNext:
You've now watched every major piece of innate immunity work — and fail. Detection. Recruitment. Engulfment. Killing. Inflammation. Systemic defense. Chronic containment.

These aren't vocabulary terms anymore. They're characters in a story.
